issue_title: Pay to scripthash
issue_body:
I have no experience with paying to scripthashes except when it's worked automagically for me before.

In #12 it looks like the initial workins of P2SH are being added. I'm not sure how straight forward P2SH is.

But... for the time being shouldn't we throw an exception if we try to `send()` to an address that does not begin with 1? Are there any sideeffects to doing so?

I think I just lost a fair bit of coin not reviewing this properly. Hopefully if we add in an exception we can keep others from doing the same.

issue_title: aiohttp mocking binary content
issue_body:
I am refactoring one of my project from using `requests` to `aiohttp` as http client library and my exists test failing.
I found 2 issues in mockig aiohttp
First is url handling, when i write test for `requests` library - pook require full url to match
```python
url = 'http://data.alexa.com/data?cli=10&dat=snbamz&url=http%3A%2F%2Ftest.org'
```
when i replace `requests` to `aiohttp` pook stop matching full url and need now url without parameters
```python
url = 'http://data.alexa.com/data'
```
Second issue with content mocking, when i use `requests` i mock binary content
```python
    mock_response_content = b'<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>\r\n\r\n<!-- Need more Alexa data?  Find our APIs here: https://aws.amazon.com/alexa/ -->\r\n<ALEXA VER="0.9" URL="test.org/" HOME="0" AID="=" IDN="test.org/">...</ALEXA>'

issue_title: passing a header dict to the write method
issue_body:
It would be very useful to be able to pass a complete header dictionary to the write method. For instnace if you read a NRRD file, then do operations on the header and then you want to save the same data with the updated header.
One of such operations is to add metadata like indicated here:
http://teem.sourceforge.net/nrrd/lib.html#keyvalue

Another operation is to save the data in a different coordinate system. In that case it would be ideal to be able to update the respective fields in the header and then write the data with the new header.

issue_title: Add support to head requests
The "r" key should toogle the reverse checkbox when used with rr.
issue_body:
##### Describe the issue
description here

##### Steps to replicate

$ rr record …
$ gdbgui --rr -r

Within Firefox, type:
> c       #  stop at the SEGV.
> b …  #  add a breakpoint
> rc      # Restart and continue instead of doing a reverse-continue.

The problem is that "r" is interpreted as the "run" command, while it should probably be a toggle for the "reverse" checkbox when --rr is used on the command line.

##### Describe your environment
* Operating system and version: NixOS
* gdbgui version (`gdbgui -v`): 0.11.1.2
* gdb version (`gdb -v`): 8.1
* rr version (`gdb -v`): 5.1.0
* browser: Firefox nightly
* python packages (`pip freeze`): command not found. (installed with Nix)

issue_title: IEEE Compressed Block ACK decoding fails
issue_body:
_From [gyiakou...@gmail.com](https://code.google.com/u/110783759787131419981/) on April 02, 2013 20:49:24_

What steps will reproduce the problem? - Try to parse the attached packet (in pcap file) using radiotap and ieee80211 headers. What is the expected output? What do you see instead? - You'll get a NeedData exception instead of the decoded packet. What version of the product are you using? On what operating system? - latest from svn at ubuntu with python 2.7 Please provide any additional information below. There are two types of block-acks, basic (with 128bytes bitmap) and compressed (with 8 bytes bitmap). Currently dpkt only works for the basic and fails when a compressed ack is met. I attach a diff that interprets compressed backs instead of basic backs, obviously different decoding is needed for each of these categories!

Thanks,
Y.

**Attachment:** [block-ack.pcap block_ack.diff](http://code.google.com/p/dpkt/issues/detail?id=105)

issue_title: `thefuck/thefuck/rules/no_command.py` is not cross-platform
issue_body:
[`thefuck/thefuck/rules/no_command.py`](https://github.com/nvbn/thefuck/blob/master/thefuck/rules/no_command.py) blindly looks for `/usr/lib/command-not-found` which does not exist on Windows, OS X, or a great many Linux distributions (Debian, for example).

I'd like to suggest a more cross-platform method of finding the correct command:
 - enumerate the list of executables (almost certainly by walking `$PATH` [or running `hash` if you have a `$SHELL` instance])
  - This would be a great chance to also run `alias` in `$SHELL` and get the list of aliases, too.
 - find the lowest Levenshtein distance from `original_command` amongst the list
 - return it

This should make this glorious project even more useful to all the sysadmins on SpecialSnowflakeCustomLinuxDistro and maybe even those stuck on Windows.

issue_title: Add other format?
issue_body:
Currently, pantable support to and from CSV.

Potentially, other table formats could be supported:

- [ ] `.xlsx`: this one will be useful but difficult:
    - Ideally, one would want to read/write `.xlsx` just like how pandoc read/write `.docx`. But there's seems no good cli to convert between `.docx` and `.xlsx` (to pass the `.docx` to pandoc).
    - So one might allow "markdown syntax" in `.xlsx`, which might works just like the current `.csv` but seems counterintuitive (people expect rich formatting in Excel).
    - Or find a tool to convert `.xlsx` to `.html` to pass the `.html` to pandoc (quite lossy though).
    - And then this' a question of what if people want to intermix rich text and markdown syntax (e.g. for LaTeX equation).
- [ ] HTML: if someone find writing the source table in HTML easier, say when it is a big table, but want to output to other formats as well
- [ ] YAML (table representing general YAML is not ideal, I'm thinking more like 2 column tables to show the key-value pairs)
- [ ] JSON (similar to above)

issue_title: Add ability to switch between "develop" locations (often local) for a dependency and the preferred (often remote) location
issue_body: The idea would be you could optionally specify a `develop` option for dependency and then, when `--develop` is passed on the command line, or read from local configuration, that location would then be used. This really only allows seamless switching between the `develop` and preferred `location` and the use case is really to allow you to develop a lot of inter-dependent repositories at the same time and switch between local and remote code. You can achieve this today be simply locally overriding the the relevant `--<dependency>-location` setting and saving in your local conf file, but this feature should make things a little easier and allow a default `develop` path to be specified in your `scontruct` file.

issue_title: Bind request fails when server returns two responses instead of one
issue_body:
With a certain LDAP server, there's an issue that for certain users it returns two responses to a single bind request. First response is "success", second is "invalidCredentials". For another user, it gives only "success" response and ldap3 library works fine with that.

I reproduced the same behavior with this server using both ldapsearch utility and this ldap3 library. The difference is that ldapsearch considers it a successful binding and can execute LDAP search commands after binding. ldap3 library thinks it's unsuccessful and refuses to continue.

I'm using ldap3==2.3. Unfortunately I don't know exact details of the LDAP server and configuration but I understood it's based on OpenLDAP 2.4.19.

Here are the requests and responses as logged by ldap3 with EXTENDED logging and fast_decoder=False:

```
LDAPMessage:
 messageID=8
 protocolOp=ProtocolOp:
  bindRequest=BindRequest:
   version=3
   name=uid=myuser,ou=People,ou=SOMETHING,o=ELSE
   authentication=AuthenticationChoice:
    simple=<stripped N characters of sensitive data>
  controls=Controls:

LDAPMessage:
 messageID=8
 protocolOp=ProtocolOp:
  bindRequest=BindResponse:
   resultCode=success
   matchedDN=
   diagnosticMessage=
   referral=Referral:
   serverSaslCreds=<stripped 10 characters of sensitive data>
  controls=Controls:

LDAPMessage:
 messageID=8
 protocolOp=ProtocolOp:
  bindRequest=BindResponse:
   resultCode=’invalidCredentials’
   matchedDN=
   diagnosticMessage=
   referral=Referral:
   serverSaslCreds=<stripped 10 characters of sensitive data>
  controls=Controls:
```

Here are requests and responses captured with tcpdump on ldapsearch

```
(real end user):

4	0.000846	222.222.222.222	111.111.111.111	LDAP	129	bindRequest(1) "uid=myuser,ou=People,ou=SOMETHING,o=ELSE" simple
6	0.097554	111.111.111.111	222.222.222.222	LDAP	80	bindResponse(1) success 
8	0.097595	111.111.111.111	222.222.222.222	LDAP	80	bindResponse(1) invalidCredentials

("service" account):

22	29.809932	222.222.222.222	111.111.111.111	LDAP	129	bindRequest(1) "cn=serviceuser,ou=SystemUsers,o=ELSE" simple 
24	29.813218	111.111.111.111	222.222.222.222	LDAP	80	bindResponse(1) success
```

For reference, I also tried end user binding with an intentionally wrong password and it returned only one response, "invalidCredentials".

Is there any way that I can configure ldap3 to treat the binding in this case as a success, like ldapsearch does? I'm even fine with some monkey patching if it's possible in this case.

issue_title: "TypeError: putenv() argument 2 must be string, not None" Error when using pac_context_for_url
issue_body:
pypac version: 0.9.0 (retrieved from pip)
Python version: Python 2.7.15 (v2.7.15:ca079a3ea3, Apr 30 2018, 16:30:26) [MSC v.1500 64 bit (AMD64)] on win32

When the configured pac file returns "DIRECT" for a URL then the function will fail with the following error:
```
  File "file.py", line 27, in get_job_info
    with pac_context_for_url('http://' + self.host):
  File "C:\Python27\lib\contextlib.py", line 17, in __enter__
    return self.gen.next()
  File "C:\Python27\lib\site-packages\pypac\api.py", line 305, in pac_context_for_url
    os.environ['HTTP_PROXY'] = proxies.get('http')
  File "C:\Python27\lib\os.py", line 422, in __setitem__
    putenv(key, item)
TypeError: putenv() argument 2 must be string, not None
```

In this case the pac file will return "DIRECT" for the url - the code gets to line 303
`proxies = resolver.get_proxy_for_requests(url)`
which will result in the code going to pypac/resolver.py:133  and the function  proxy_parameter_for_requests("DIRECT") will be called. This will then set proxy_url_or_direct to None which will be used for the returns values in the dictionary for 'http' and 'https'. These value of None are used in
```
os.environ['HTTP_PROXY'] = proxies.get('http')
os.environ['HTTPS_PROXY'] = proxies.get('https')
```
Resulting in the error putenv() argument 2 must be string, not None

issue_title: ANALYZE features speeds computation up to 1000x ?
issue_body:
Hi. 
I've been using gffutis with gencode v18 human genome annotation(http://www.gencodegenes.org/releases/current.html)
and noticed the following:
if I create database this way:
```
#!/usr/bin/python2
import gffutils as gff
import sys
db = gff.create_db('gencode.v19.annotation.gff3',
                   'gencode.v19.annotation.gff3.db',
                   force=True,
                   merge_strategy="merge")
```
And then run multiple calls to db.parent:
```
#!/usr/bin/python2
import gffutils as gff
import os.path
import sys

db = gff.FeatureDB('gencode.v19.annotation.gff3.db')
with open(os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), '1000_transcripts.txt')) as transcripts:
        for t in transcripts:
                t = t.strip()
                for gene in db.parents(t, featuretype='gene'):
                        print gene
```
1000_transcripts.txt contains 1000 random transcripts to human genome.
The computation time of the second piece of code from `time` bash command appears to be
```
real    0m31.850s
user    0m17.351s
sys     0m14.497s
```
But if I create database this way:
```
#!/usr/bin/python2
import gffutils as gff
import sys
db = gff.create_db('gencode.v19.annotation.gff3',
                   'gencode.v19.annotation.gff3.db',
                   force=True,
                   merge_strategy="merge")
db.execute('ANALYZE features')
```
Then multiple calls to parent take
```
real    0m0.089s
user    0m0.072s
sys     0m0.016s
```

However I can't see this effect if call to parent is performed in the same script as database creation.
I suggest #55 as a fix.

issue_title: Hard coded remote names
issue_body:
I've only started playing, and it seems like the remote 'gitlab' is special, and everything else is ignored.  I suspect the same occurs for github and others.

This means `git-repo` cant be immediately used in a git clone of a gitlab repo that was cloned using `git clone`, as that will use the remote name `origin`.

It would be nice if `git-repo` handled a missing `gitlab` remote by looking for another suitable remote that points to gitlab, with a sensible strategy that works in most cases.

The most obvious fallback is to look to see if `origin` is a gitlab repo, and use that.

Then looking at all remotes, and see if there is only one that points to gitlab, then that should be the default used.

`git-repo` could then create a `gitlab` remote if it needs to avoid expensive calls again, so the `gitlab` remote becomes more of a cached default remote, rather than the only one that works.

issue_title: Validity of bounding box after cell deep copy
issue_body:
`gdspy.Cell.copy()` provides the ability to create a deep copy of a cell. It was noticed that when this is done for nested cells, any `CellReferences` within the cell have a their `_bb_valid` flags set to `True`. Hence, if you perform a deep copy of a cell, and then try to get its bounding box using `get_bounding_box()`, the algorithm fails, generating a `KeyError` at the return statement of `gdspy.Cell.get_bounding_box()` method.

I do not know all the implications of this, but at the very least, I believe the `_bb_valid` flags for the dependencies have to be explicitly set to `False` within `cell.copy()` to allow recalculation of the bounding boxes: 
```
for ref in new_cell.get_dependencies(True):
            if ref._bb_valid:
                print('Reference ' + str(ref) + ' bb_valid flag is True, set to False')
                ref._bb_valid = False
```

issue_title: Use Travis CI
issue_body:
Since you already have tests it is generally a good idea to have [Travis CI](https://travis-ci.org/craffel/mir_eval) to actually run them for every commit and pull request.

A simple `.travis.yml` could look like (not tested)

    language: python
    python:
        - "2.7"
        - "3.3"
        - "3.4"
    virtualenv:
        system_site_packages: true
    install:
        - sudo apt-get update -qq
        - if [[ $TRAVIS_PYTHON_VERSION == *"2."* ]]; then sudo apt-get install python-numpy python-scipy; fi
        - if [[ $TRAVIS_PYTHON_VERSION == *"3."* ]]; then sudo apt-get install python3-numpy python3-scipy; fi
        - pip install -e .
        - pip install nosetests sphinx
    script:
        - nosetests
        - python setup.py build_sphinx
        - python setup.py egg_info -b.dev sdist --formats gztar

issue_title: cpu_freq does not work in (at least) Linux Ubuntu 16.04 and Mint 17.2
issue_body:
Trying to import `cpu_freq` from `psutil` (latest - 5.1.3) on Linux Mint 17.2 fails. The function does not get defined because there is no `/sys/devices/system/cpu/cpufreq` on the system (as per [this line](https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/blob/76d1fb61c14d286aa645f154f4a2b7a7bae8a828/psutil/_pslinux.py#L639))

There is, however, `/sys/devices/system/cpu/cpu0/cpufreq` (and for cpu1, cpu2..)

On Ubuntu 16.04 (at least this one machine), there is `/sys/devices/system/cpu/cpufreq` but it is empty and therefore `cpu_freq()` just returns an empty list

issue_title: Conventions used for quaternions and Euler angles
issue_body:
Can you please clarify the convention used for Quaternions and Euler angles?

Are you using the classic Euler angles or Tait–Bryan angles?
And as for Quaternions, are you using the JPL convention (Left handed, i.e i*j = -k) or the classic Hamiltonian convention (Right handed, i.e i*j = k)? Maybe you can add this to the README page?

issue_title: freezegun crashes with cryptography 1.2 because of non-integer dict keys
issue_body:
Hello,

I originally filed this on cryptography, but they said it was probably more appropriate for freezegun or cffi.  Please let me know if you think it's more appropriate for cffi.  Thank you!

Cheers,
Fitz

Original bug report:
----

With the release of cryptography-1.2.0, my freezegun-using tests have started crashing.  The traceback looks like:

````
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "breaker2.py", line 10, in <module>
    with freezegun.freeze_time('2015-10-31 12:00:01'):
  File "/Users/fitz/.virtualenvs/cgdb/lib/python3.4/site-packages/freezegun/api.py", line 321, in __enter__
    self.start()
  File "/Users/fitz/.virtualenvs/cgdb/lib/python3.4/site-packages/freezegun/api.py", line 374, in start
    for module_attribute in dir(module):
TypeError: unorderable types: str() < int()
````

The crash occurs on [this line](https://github.com/spulec/freezegun/blob/master/freezegun/api.py#L375) when freezegun attempts to `dir()` on `_cffi_backend._extern_py`.  That module has integer keys in its `__dict__` method, causing the above error.  The dump of the `__dict__` looks like this:

````
{4441887152: (<ctype 'void(*)(int, int, char *, int)'>,
              <bound method type._lock_cb of <class 'cryptography.hazmat.bindings.openssl.binding.Binding'>>,
              b'\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00',
              None),
 4441887184: (<ctype 'int(*)(char *, int, int, void *)'>,
              <function _pem_password_cb at 0x1077e3488>,
              b'\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00',
              None),
 4441887216: (<ctype 'int(*)(unsigned char *, int)'>,
              <function _osrandom_rand_bytes at 0x108b6f2f0>,
              b'\xff\xff\xff\xff\x00\x00\x00\x00',
              None),
 4441887248: (<ctype 'int(*)()'>,
              <function _osrandom_rand_status at 0x108b6f378>,
              b'\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00',
              None),
 '__doc__': None,
 '__loader__': None,
 '__name__': '_cffi_backend._extern_py',
 '__package__': None,
 '__spec__': None}
````

The suspect commit is 830af271de1c9c358, when cryptography was updated to use cffi's static callbacks.  [Here is a sample script that demonstrates the breakage.](https://gist.github.com/felliott/4547366a649dac4df6d1)

I'm relatively new to Python, so I'm not sure if this is a bug in cffi, cryptography, or freezegun. I'm happy to refile elsewhere if needed.  Please let me know if you need any more information.  Thank you very much and have a great day!

Cheers,
Fitz

issue_title: find a small bug when using NLA type : 'array'.
issue_body:
**I find a bug when using the pre-defined NLA type : 'array'.**
**in 'pyroute2/netlink/__init__.py', line 1741:**
```
         def encode(self):
              fmt = '%s%i%s' % (self.fmt[:-1], len(self.value), self.fmt[-1:])
             self['value'] = struct.pack(fmt, self.value)
              nla_base.encode(self)
```

**fixed:**
```
         def encode(self):
              fmt = '%s%i%s' % (self.fmt[:-1], len(self.value), self.fmt[-1:])
             self['value'] = struct.pack(fmt, *self.value)
              nla_base.encode(self)
```

issue_title: [rewrite] FFmpegPCMAudio's pipe mode fails for internet-originating streams
issue_body:
**tl;dr** `FFmpegPCMAudio`'s pipe mode will not play audio from an internet source because `subprocess.Popen` mangles the data

I've spent almost a whole day trying to figure out the exact cause of this problem. I'm opening this issue so that others know that this limitation exists and is not caused by discord.py. The problem first occurred when I tried to write/run code that looked a little bit like this.

```python
voice = some_voice_channel.connect() # connect the bot to a voice channel

...

import urllib3
http = urllib3.PoolManager()

# open a stream request to a foreign audio resource
response = http.request('GET', 'http://example.com/audioresource.wav', preload_content=False)
# use pipe mode to direct the bytestream directly to the ffmpeg process
voice.play( discord.FFmpegPCMAudio(response, pipe=True), after=response.close() )
```

The goal here being that you can open a HTTP connection using urllib3 and pipe the download stream directly into the underlying ffmpeg process, and *should* be effectively equivalent to this sample, using a file handle instead of a network stream:

```python
voice = some_voice_channel.connect()

...

audiofile = open('audioresource.wav', 'rb')
voice.play( discord.FFmpegPCMAudio(audiofile, pipe=True), after=audiofile.close )
```
which I can confirm works perfectly.

From what I have determined so far, `subprocess.Popen` outputs mangled data when an stdin pipe's source has a non-trivial latency (such as the latency associated with an online file transfer). As further proof to this, the HTTP stream example does execute correctly if the resource is hosted on a local HTTP server (say, with `python -m SimpleHTTPServer`, and passing in `http://192.168.1.1/audioresource.wav`).

I think this should be mentioned in the documentation, since it takes quite a lot of digging to determine that this limitation is **not** caused by discord.py.

--------

I did develop a workaround for this problem, however it is fairly... it's a hack, pretty much.

```python
voice = some_voice_channel.connect() # connect the bot to a voice channel

...

import urllib3, subprocess
http = urllib3.PoolManager()

# open a stream request to a foreign audio resource
response = http.request('GET', 'http://example.com/audioresource.wav', preload_content=False)
player = discord.FFmpegPCMAudio(subprocess.PIPE, pipe=True)

def download_redirect(resp, process):
    while 1:
        chunk = resp.read(8192)
        if not chunk:
            resp.close()
            process.stdin.close() # tells ffmpeg that there's no more input, triggering voice to call the 'after' callback
            break
        process.stdin.write(chunk)

download_thread = threading.Thread(target=download_redirect, args=(response, player._process))

voice.play( player, after=response.close )
download_thread.start()
```

So we create the FFmpegPCMAudio player and give it a subprocess.PIPE, which eventually becomes the stdin argument in a `Popen` call ([documentation](https://docs.python.org/3/library/subprocess.html#subprocess.Popen)). Then I open a thread to read data from the response and write it directly to the ffmpeg process' stdin pipe.

issue_title: WebSocket ratelimiting cause the client to exit
issue_body:
If the client sends too many messages in a short amount of time on the WebSocet connection (for example by sending status updates too often), it will be terminated with the data "rate limited".  The current rate limit on the WebSocet is 60/60 according to the discord devs.

The debug logs shows the following appearing:

    DEBUG:websockets.protocol:client << Frame(fin=True, opcode=8, data=b'\x0f\xa8rate limited')
    DEBUG:websockets.protocol:client >> Frame(fin=True, opcode=8, data=b'\x0f\xa8rate limited')

The `Client.connect()` method then exits with no errors logged or exceptions raised, making it hard to track down what happened.

issue_title: Python 2.x permits comparison between Money and string
issue_body:
In Python 2.x you can compare numeric and non-numeric types. This means you can do:

```python
>>> Money('3', 'GBP') > '50'
False
```

which can lead to confusing situations (e.g., accidentally forgotten to deserialise the `'50'` to a numeric value). It seems to me that this situation should be rejected in Python 2.x. In Python 3.x the above leads to a `TypeError`:

```python
>>> Money('3', 'GBP') > '50'
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/Users/svisser/.virtualenvs/test3/lib/python3.4/site-packages/money/money.py", line 75, in __gt__
    return self.amount > other
TypeError: unorderable types: decimal.Decimal() > str()
```

issue_title: GDAX L3 Book Deltas
issue_body:
I'm trying to obtain Book deltas (I want as unprocessed a feed of order book updates as possible). I'm using the following code:

````
async def bookdelta(feed, pair, delta):
    print('Feed: {} Pair: {} adds: {} dels: {} upd: {}'.format(feed, pair, len(delta[BID][ADD]), len(delta[BID][DEL]), len(delta[BID][UPD]))) 

f.add_feed(GDAX(pairs=['BTC-USD'], channels=[L3_BOOK], callbacks={BOOK_DELTA: BookUpdateCallback(bookdelta)}))
````

however the callback never seems to get called. Running the L3_BOOK channel with the BookCallback does work but I don't want the updated orderbook, I'm trying to get the individual update messages for the order book.

issue_title: repeated options; better list handling
issue_body:
Hi guys! Great module!

What I really want in my config file is:

```
--device pc2000  192.168.0.194 mysql user_maintainer 12984710289471234324732144321432
--device pc3000  192.168.0.198 mysql user_maintainer 12984710289471234324732144321432
--device device1 192.168.0.195 mysql user_maintainer 12984710289471234324732144321432
--device device2 192.168.0.196 mysql user_maintainer 12984710289471234324732144321432
--device device3 192.168.0.197 mysql user_maintainer 12984710289471234324732144321432
--device device4 192.168.0.198 mysql user_maintainer 12984710289471234324732144321432
--device device5 192.168.0.201 mysql user_maintainer 12984710289471234324732144321432
--device device6 192.168.0.200 mysql user_maintainer 12984710289471234324732144321432
```
What I need to do currently because I am not able to specify the same option repeatedly plus the special treatment of spaces/commas:

```
--device [pc2000|192.168.0.194|mysql|user_maintainer|12984710289471234324732144321432, pc3000|192.168.0.198|mysql|user_maintainer|12984710289471234324732144321432, device1|192.168.0.195|mysql|user_maintainer|12984710289471234324732144321432, device2|192.168.0.196|mysql|user_maintainer|12984710289471234324732144321432, device3|192.168.0.197|mysql|user_maintainer|12984710289471234324732144321432, device4|192.168.0.198|mysql|user_maintainer|12984710289471234324732144321432, device5|192.168.0.201|mysql|user_maintainer|12984710289471234324732144321432, device6|192.168.0.200|mysql|user_maintainer|12984710289471234324732144321432]
```

plus this code

```
options.device = [device.Device(*d.split("|")) for d in options.device]
```

This makes my config file so much more unreadable.
Could this somehow be improved? i.e. can I get repeated options and better list handling?

Best Regards
Michael

issue_title: Dataset delete attribute fails for CommandGroupLength element
issue_body: [Dataset.\__delattr__(name)](https://github.com/darcymason/pydicom/blob/26e769b63a12eb3295588961ed22a81643aa9de2/pydicom/dataset.py#L174) generates a tag from an element name and then uses this tag to check if its non-zero and in the dataset prior to deleting it, but the tag for CommandGroupLength is (0x0000, 0x0000) and hence fails the check and so can't be deleted with this method.

issue_title: DataFrame to LASfile
issue_body:
The fact that lasio allows for easy manipulation via pandas is really fantastic (#94).

If this functionality doesn't already exist in lasio, it would be excellent if any changes that get applied to the `l.df` could be "pushed" back into the LASfile object.

That is, say for instance that I manipulate the `l.df` DataFrame object (in [the df notebook example](lasio/notebooks/using lasio with pandas DataFrame.ipynb), for example, `l.df.RES` is created), it could be useful to then "push" those changes back into the `LASfile.data` attribute, where it could be written out as an las file as needed.

issue_title: Lazy rendering and transform.save() issue
issue_body:
Greetings. Seems like creating message from template for later rendering and applying transformers doesn't work one with another? Like I see all other message methods, for example dkim signature works lazy, so I can do something like this:
```
msg = Message(subject=Template(), html=Template())
msg.dkim(**dkim_options)
```
and later `msg.send(to, render=context)`
But transformer.save() modifies not rendered template, but `template.template_text` before rendering (for example this would not work properly with extending template from base).
I should not use template feature in this case, or what's the proper way to do this?
Also maybe there should be method of Message to implement, that can modify message after template rendering? Thanks for your help and ideas...
Really cool lib, by the way. Thanks

issue_title: pyodbc insert/update failure with freetds on SQL Server 2014
issue_body:
- Python: 3.4.2
- pyodbc: 4.0.22
- OS: Raspbian
- DB: Microsoft SQL Server 2014
- driver: FreeTDS

### Issue

I have created a stored procedure for issuing inventory. I'm using this in conjunction with a Raspberry Pi and barcode scanner.

I've tested this on my Macbook and it works fine using pyodbc and freetds driver. However, it fails when executing under a scenario that affects a certain table.

This stored procedure, under a certain scenario, requires the execution of another stored procedure (which is basically just an insert statement). I've tried removing this nested stored procedure and replacing it with the insert statement the stored procedure was calling. This didn't seem to help, so I commented out the code and tried to randomly update the table, and again it failed.

The message I'm receiving is INSERT failed because the following SET options have incorrect settings "CONCAT_NULL_YIELDS_NULL, ANSI_WARNINGS, ANSI_PADDING." Verify that SET options are correct.....

Alternatively, I get the same message starting with UPDATE when trying to use the update statement.

I've tried recreating the stored procedure with turning those on and I've also edited the odbc.ini file on the Raspberry Pi to set AnsiNPW and QuotedID to YES.

I'm not even sure if this could be a pyodbc issue, I'm thinking it could be a misuse of T-SQL on my part or something with the FreeTDS driver.

Below is my code, after the @pWOBOMID evaluating for 0 is where the issues begin. You can see where I've commented out the insert in an attempt to retry using the stored procedure. Again, this is working fine on my mac but not on the raspberry pi.

Thanks for any help!

AS

		RETURN(2)  --we should return a message here

	FROM [Work Order BOM]
	WHERE WOID = @pWOID AND ProductID = @pProductID

	IF @pWOBOMID = 0

		EXEC dbo.sprocNewIT 3, @pDate, DEFAULT, @pProductID, @pQtyToIssue, 0, DEFAULT, DEFAULT, @pWOID, DEFAULT, @pEmpID, @pTransactionID = @pInvTxID OUTPUT
		--BEGIN

		--	VALUES (3, @pDate, @pProductID, CAST(@pQtyToIssue AS numeric(18,3)), @pWOID, @pEmpID)

		--	SELECT @pInvTxID = @@IDENTITY
		--END

		INSERT INTO [Work Order BOM] ([WOID], [ProductID], [Qty Required], [Qty Issued], [Qty Remaining], [Complete], [Transaction ID])
		VALUES (@pWOID, @pProductID, @pQtyToIssue, @pQtyToIssue, 0, 1, @pInvTxID)

		SELECT @pWOBOMID = @@IDENTITY


--CREATE WOBOMISSUE TRANSACTION
	INSERT INTO [Work Order BOM Issues] ([WOBOM ID], [Issue Date], [Employee ID], [Qty Issued])
	VALUES (@pWOBOMID, @pDate, @pEmpID, @pQtyToIssue)

	SELECT @pWOBOMIssueID = @@IDENTITY

--MODIFY HOLD TRANSACTION IN OLD INVENTORY TRANSACTIONS TABLE
	--UPDATE [Inventory Transactions]
	--SET [Quantity] = isnull([Quantity], 0) - @pQtyToIssue, [Transaction Modified Date] = @pDate
	--WHERE [Transaction ID] = @pInvTxID

--GET FIFO LAYER dATA
	DECLARE cursor_fifo_layers CURSOR Read_Only

	FOR 
	SELECT [ID], [UnitCost], [QtyOnHand]

	WHERE ([QtyOnHand] > 0 AND [ProductID] = @pProductID)
	ORDER BY [CreatedDate], [ID]

	OPEN cursor_fifo_layers

		FETCH NEXT FROM cursor_fifo_layers
		INTO @pFIFOLayerID, @pFIFOUnitCost, @pFIFOQtyOnHand  --This drops you into the 1st record of the recordset (cursor)

		While @@FETCH_STATUS = 0  --W1 --This monitors for the last record of the recordset
		
			BEGIN

				SELECT @pRemoveUntil = @pFIFOQtyOnHand - @pQtyToIssue
				SELECT @pI = 0, @pComplete = 0

				IF @pRemoveUntil < 0 SELECT @pRemoveUntil = 0

issue_title: Can not insert NULL with fast_executemany = True and UNICODE string (possible memory corruption)
issue_body:
Please first make sure you have looked at:

* Documentation: https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/wiki
* Other issues

### Environment

To diagnose, we usually need to know the following, including version numbers.  On Windows, be
sure to specify 32-bit Python or 64-bit:

- Python: 2.7.14
- pyodbc: 4.0.23
- OS: Windows 10/64 Bit
- DB: Microsoft SQL Server 2016 (RTM-GDR) (KB4019088) - 13.0.1742.0 (X64)
- driver: SQL Server 10.00.16299.15

### Issue

I ran into problems when inserting datasets with unicode strings. It seems that a memory corruption occurs in this case, as depending on the instance, python itself also crashes and the arrays contain data which can not be there, and/or creates different error messages.
In some cases it even worked, but 90% it doesn not (using always the same dataset as input).

I finally tracked it down to a minimal self contained sample which exhibits the problem.

The error which I get in this example is that I can not insert NULL values into the tables. I provided two testcases, where one works and the other not. The only difference is that the non-working sample uses a unicode string.

Interestingly, by allowing NULL inserts on the Link table I could also reproduce the behaviour from
https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/issues/380

[error_NULL_allowed.log](https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/files/2026136/error_NULL_allowed.log)
[import_with NULL_allowed.txt](https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/files/2026141/import_with.NULL_allowed.txt)
so they might be related.

[error.log](https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/files/2026109/error.log)

[import.txt](https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/files/2026122/import.txt)

issue_title: Recreating Gavin's MultiSig TwoOfThree.sh referance implimentation.
issue_body:
Hi All,

I am trying (and filing) to recreate the referance multisig addresss from Gavin (https://gist.github.com/gavinandresen/3966071) .

It is my fault but I would like some pointers if possible, the code I am using is;-


    from pycoin.tx.pay_to import ScriptMultisig, address_for_pay_to_script

    keys = [
        "0491bba2510912a5bd37da1fb5b1673010e43d2c6d812c514e91bfa9f2eb129e1c183329db55bd868e209aac2fbc02cb33d98fe74bf23f0c235d6126b1d8334f86",
        "04865c40293a680cb9c020e7b1e106d8c1916d3cef99aa431a56d253e69256dac09ef122b1a986818a7cb624532f062c1d1f8722084861c5c3291ccffef4ec6874",
         "048d2455d2403e08708fc1f556002f1b6cd83f992d085097f9974ab08a28838f07896fbab08f39495e15fa6fad6edbfb1e754e35fa1c7844c41f322a1863d46213",
    ]

    generated_address = address_for_pay_to_script(ScriptMultisig(2, keys).script())
    from_page ='3QJmV3qfvL9SuYo34YihAf3sRCW3qSinyC'

    if generated_address == from_page:
        print('It worked')
    else:
        print('You fail')

In the above instance I am getting back 3FB66msrsz6egVRue8JxnUfXPW8nACQsr6 and not the expected 3QJmV3qfvL9SuYo34YihAf3sRCW3qSinyC.

Needless to say, I am getting a log of "You fail" messages.  I suspect it is because I am not encoding the sec keys properly but for the life of my I cannot work out how I should be doing it.

Any chance for a friendly tip?

Neil

issue_title: More Sphinx 1.6 problems
issue_body:
Sort of a follow-on from #71, which prompted me to dig deeper into how we were not really compatible with Sphinx 1.5/1.6.

Sphinx 1.5 was just due to Python 2.6 being dropped, so we did that here too, and no problem.

Sphinx 1.6 made a lot of internal API changes that broke us. I fixed all the stuff that seems to pop up in our test suites, but there's a side channel - parsing the changelog 'in-process' for stuff like my release Invoke tasks (which need to answer questions about a project's changelog state.)

Specifically, something seems to be almost skipping Sphinx itself when rendering, leaving us with "I'm docutils and what is this???" errors like:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6088/31794592-ac84acc0-b4d7-11e7-9e66-b740e35eb8ce.png)

Well...clearly `:doc:` should freaking work in Sphinx-flavored RST! (It also complained about a `:ref:` that I temporarily commented out.)

issue_title: Cannot create a rule to match manually-created rules
issue_body:
I'm looking for a compact method to find a specific rule in a chain. I tried to do this by creating a rule with certain characteristics and then running:

    idx = chain.rules.index(rule)

This approach fails because the equality test fails in the Match class. The following code illustrates the problem:

```
import iptc

table = iptc.Table(iptc.Table.FILTER)
chain = iptc.Chain(table, 'RH-Firewall-1-INPUT')

m1 = chain.rules[5].matches[0]
print 'm1: name=%s, state=%s, match.u.user.name=%s' % (m1.name, m1.state, m1.match.u.user.name)

m2 = iptc.Match(chain.rules[5], name='state')
m2.state = 'NEW'
print 'm2: name=%s, state=%s, match.u.user.name=%s' % (m2.name, m2.state, m2.match.u.user.name)

if m1 == m2:
    print "m1 is not equal to m2"
else:
    print "m1 is not equal to m2"

```
On my system, this code prints:

```
m1: name=state, state=NEW, match.u.user.name=state
m2: name=state, state=NEW, match.u.user.name=conntrack
m1 is not equal to m2

```
This is on a Centos 7 system running kernel version 3.10.0-693.2.2.el7 and iptables-1.4.21-16.el7.x86_64.

The 'conntrack' value is coming from these lines in Match.__init__:

```
module = self._xt.find_match(name)
real_name = module and getattr(module[0], 'real_name', None) or None

```

issue_title: About pandas data feed--No atrribute "_barstack" error
Crashes when rendering tables with headings
issue_body:
Broken in mistletoe 0.4.1. Spotted by @cctile in #10. Derp.

The cause is that since 0.4.1 `token.children` returns a list (or tuple), and `HTMLRenderer` and `LaTeXRenderer` tries to call `next` on the list (or tuple).

I could patch it the easy way, but the neater solution will change the `Table` class slightly, and make future `render_table` functions less gross.

issue_title: Fix nightmarish parsing configuration with a more elegant solution
issue_body:
The way parsing is handled for the core tests is a *nightmare* and seriously impedes the ability to extend pyresttest.  We have a special case for each parse option, and the handling of command-line, test-level, and testset options is completely bonkers, since each parses differently. 

Let's apply lessons learnt to fix this.   Instead of the long, brutish mess of parsing (and test of the parsing), let's apply the lessons learned in the validator classes, and apply a registry + dynamic parse function method.  But, *let's do it better* since type coercion is required.  

Use registries:

```python
RESERVED_KEYWORDS = set('test', 'blah', 'bleh')  # Cannot be used as a macro name
MACROS = {test: parse_test, benchmark: parse_benchmark}  # Consider carefully
STEPS = {}  # Currently not used, used when run methods get subdivided
OPTIONS = {   # Can occur at global, macro, or testset level
  'name': (coerce_function, type, default, duplicate_allowed)
}
```

Need to think about options, but items will be iteratively run through each dictionary looking for matches and then handed to parse functions.  For options, if duplicates are added, they'll be combined together.

For the command-line ("global") arguments, they need to get mapped to an option too, but it'll be more flexible. 

This also acts as a useful and compatibility-maintaining precursor to the nested context structure from https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/issues/45.

issue_title: licensing contradiction
issue_body:
I think it's a bit of an old fad, but dual licensing doesn't make much sense. jQuery did it and all of a sudden projects came after dual-licensing. [They ended up backing out of it](https://blog.jquery.com/2012/09/10/jquery-licensing-changes/).

There's no benefit to using GPL as a license if the user can just pick MIT. There's no point in keeping an MIT license if the provisions of GPL are active. It's best to go with one or the other.

issue_title: Issue with add_table
issue_body:
add_table throws a misleading warning in the variant of add_table that uses integer columns and rows when the ranges are setup incorrectly.  Given this, an error is the expected output but the type of error has caused undue headaches on my part given that the error thrown tells me nothing about why it was actually failing in a much more complicated context.

This is on Python 2.7.6 using xlsxwriter 0.9.3 and Excel 2016.

Demo code:

``` python

import xlsxwriter

workbook = xlsxwriter.Workbook("test.xlsx")
sheet = workbook.add_worksheet()
table = {"name": "Test", "data": [], "columns": [ {"header": "One"} ]}

# The table range is incorrect, so an error should be thrown but the wrong one is thrown
sheet.add_table(0, 0, 1, 1, table)

workbook.close()
```

This is what's thrown:

> /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/xlsxwriter/worksheet.py:2361: UserWarning: Duplicate header name in add_table(): 'one'
> % force_unicode(name))
> -1

What it should throw (I'm just filling in what I think it should say):

> /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/xlsxwriter/worksheet.py:2361: UserWarning: Manually specified table bounds in add_table doesn't line up with the dimensions of the data given!

issue_title: Consider using `placebo` for mocking AWS calls
issue_body:
There is a package (by Mitch Garnaat - initial author of `boto`) named [`placebo`](https://github.com/garnaat/placebo). It claims:

> Placebo allows you to mock boto3 calls that look just like normal calls but actually have no effect at all. It does this by allowing you to record a set of calls and save them to a data file and then replay those calls later (e.g. in a unit test) without ever hitting the AWS endpoints.

To me it looks like good candidate for replacing our current manually made AWS responses which are not complete in the content.

## Thinking of possible results

- using `placebo`, we once record calls to `awslogs` AWS service, save it and use later on in unit tests.
- our unittest would use `placebo` to create recorded AWS responses directly by "sending log records" to AWS. Recorded calls (and responses) would be used in unittest. Having this done with each test run would make much easier to modify log records and see they are really present.
- using `placebo` recorded calls we would create a fixture, which would build "recorded" responses from log records. This is somewhere in the half way between previous two options.

In all cases, we would be using some (really recorded or manually shaped) recorded calls/responses in our tests. The replay process would probably use `placebo`.

## Proposed steps

- initial exploration of `placebo` - does it work at all? How it records and how it replays calls.
- explore `placebo` for `awslogs` related calls. Does it meet our expectations? Does it work in this case at all?
- decide how to use `placebo`.

If all goes well, we could continue:

- replace existing in-line generated AWS responses by `placebo.pill`
- consider generating "recorded calls" at setup/fixture creation time to allow testing input log records to output log records.

The second will lead to dropped connections on reorg like we used to have during new blocks.

issue_title: `arrows` argument name is not correct
issue_body:
Having an `arrows` argument of the `chess.svg.board()` function is not correct, because we can draw a circle with the same argument (and a circle is not an arrow). I therefore suggest we rename this argument to `markings`. Or if you disagree, we could add an argument named `circles`. Shall we vote?

I am `+1` for the `markings` argument.

issue_title: Converting TeX source to html
issue_body:
Does pandoc-eqnos work when converting a TeX file to another format, such as html or epub?  In other words, does pandoc-eqnos only work when converting markdown source?
 
I use standard TeX syntax \ref{eq:gaussian} and \label{eq:gaussian} in my TeX source file and it compiles without error using TexStudio on iOS.  However, the reference and label are not converted when use pandoc with the pandoc-eqnos filter.

issue_title: cpuinfo.get_cpu_info() causes script to run twice in Windows
issue_body:
The following script reproduces the problem:

```
import cpuinfo
cpuinfo.get_cpu_info()
print 'How many times should I see this?'
```

gives output

```
How many times should I see this?
How many times should I see this?
```

I believe the problem was introduced in https://github.com/workhorsy/py-cpuinfo/commit/9bbd7fb8adb30c1cfb4389dd696a758d9d1f172f and is because the function ``_get_cpu_info_from_cpuid`` uses multiprocessing (which is problematic on Windows).

issue_title: Odd behavior when making `__wrapped__` a property
issue_body:
Hi Graham,

I just found some odd behavior when making `__wrapped__` a property. The error shows up only when using the compiled C module though. When I delete the `.so`, the behavior is as expected. Consider this small example:

```python
import wrapt

class Wrapper(wrapt.ObjectProxy):

    def __init__(self):
        self._self_data = None

        super(Wrapper, self).__init__([4, 5, 6, 7])

        self.__wrapped__ = [1, 2, 3]

    @property
    def __wrapped__(self):
        return self._self_data

    @__wrapped__.setter
    def __wrapped__(self, data):
        self._self_data = data

    def __repr__(self):
        return repr(self.__wrapped__)


w = Wrapper()
print("repr: {!r}".format(w))
print("len : {}".format(len(w)))
```

It should print

```
repr: [1, 2, 3]
len : 3
```

but with the compiled version, it outputs

```
repr: [1, 2, 3]
len : 4
```

Can you reproduce this?

Thanks!

issue_title: Check for subadapter agreement in MultiLogicAdapter
issue_body:
ChatterBot's MultiLogicAdapter module makes it possible to get a response based the output of any number of logic adapters. Currently, ChatterBot returns the response with the highest confidence value. 

One concept I am considering is the idea that if multiple adapters agree on the same statement, then that statement is more likely to be the correct response.

There are a few ways this could be implemented.
1. The first is to simply add a check to see if any adapters have returned the same response.
2. The second requires a greater number of changes to implement. This would involve redesigning logic adapters as a pipeline process. This means that each adapter would be in charge of removing a number of responses from a set of possible correct responses before passing that set to the next logic adapter. One advantage is that this would reduce some repetitive queries that are currently made in multiple logic adapters. One disadvantage is that the pipeline is not a process that could be made to run in parallel in the future.

issue_title: Capture exceptions outside of spiders
issue_body:
`scrapy-sentry` currently does not capture exceptions that happen outside the spider's `on_error` signal. This limits the usefulness of the extension. Luckily, It is pretty easy to fix as well. I currently have this in my Scrapy `settings.py` instead of configuring the extension:

```
    from twisted.python import log
    from raven import Client

    client = Client(dsn=SENTRY_DSN)

    def log_sentry(dictionary):
        if dictionary.get('isError'):
            failure = dictionary.get('failure')
            try:
                # Raise the failure
                failure.raiseException()
            except:
                # so we can capture it here.
                client.captureException()


    log.addObserver(log_sentry)
```

It should be pretty easy to do the same in the extension, and possibly remove the `on_error` handling. @llonchj, if you agree, I'm happy to prepare a PR for that.

Above snippet is not my work, it was discovered by @elmkarami in http://stackoverflow.com/questions/25262765/handle-all-exception-in-scrapy-with-sentry and https://github.com/scrapy/scrapy/issues/852.

issue_title: Doc for set_triggers_for_all_channels should specify digital channels
issue_body:
The documentation for set_triggers_for_all_channels should specify "digital" channels for each of the mentions of channels. For example if you have the following channel config:

analog_channels = [0,1,2,3,4,5,6]
digital_channels = [7]
 
Then you should only pass *one* trigger parameter into set_triggers_for_all_channels, just for the ditgital channel, even though there are a total of 8 active channels. Ideally this function would call get_active_channels and return a helpful error that says you only need to pass triggers for X digital channels.

issue_title: Symbols with non-alphanumeric characters fail
issue_body:
The following request will fail:

```python

from iexfinance imort Stock

aig = Stock("aig+")

# iexfinance.utils.exceptions.IEXSymbolError: Symbol AIG+ not found.
```

per the [IEX docs](https://iextrading.com/developer/docs/#http-methods):

> Be sure to url-encode the values you pass to your parameter.
(i.e. ?symbols=AIG+ encoded is ?symbols=AIG%2b.)

since we are not doing so now, we are essentially passing:

> symbols=AIG+

which will not work. Need to url-encode all symbol names to force percent-encodings of non-alphanum characters.

```
class User(AbstractBaseUser, PermissionsMixin):
    pk_user = UUIDField(auto=True, primary_key=True, serialize=True, hyphenate=True)
    email = models.EmailField(_('email address'), max_length=254, unique=True)

issue_title: No named URL resolution in Django
issue_body:
The included Django `auth` app allows for using named URLs in the settings, like so:

    LOGIN_REDIRECT_URL = 'my_url'

However, trying to do the same in `python-social-auth` produces an error because the named URL isn't resolved when the Django strategy retrieves it from the settings.

issue_title: remote DNS resolve has not work with websocket
issue_body:
Hi
the library will call _create_direct_connection() when trying to resolve the url, and a local resolved IP address will be passed to the socks5 connector, so the socks5 will ignore the `rdns` param because the host for the socks5 is already an ip address.


the aiosocksy doesn't have this bug.

issue_title: [feature request] custom comments in django admin (e.g. within save_form())
issue_body:
First of all: thank you for creating and maintaining such a nice OS package! :+1: 

I recently stumbled over setting custom version comments from within the django admin after migrating some admin actions into the ModelAdmin(/AdminForm). Since the django admin creates a "changed elements" message and calls log_change, custom comments from precursor methods like save_form() get overwritten.

I've come up with a quick workaround, but something nicer/more robust would be neat, especially a request-based custom comment that avoids the possibility of overwriting a concurrent save() on the same model admin instance.

```
class VersionCommentAdmin(VersionAdmin):
    """ modified VersionAdmin to allow reversion comments from within admin actions (e.g. save_form()) """

    custom_save_message = None

    def log_addition(self, request, object, change_message=None):
        # fixme: this is not thread safe, concurrent requests may overwrite comments...
        if self.custom_save_message:
            change_message = self.custom_save_message
            self.custom_save_message = None
        super(VersionCommentAdmin, self).log_addition(request, object, change_message=change_message)

    def log_change(self, request, object, message):
        # fixme: this is not thread safe, concurrent requests may overwrite comments...
        if self.custom_save_message:
            message = self.custom_save_message
            self.custom_save_message = None
        super(VersionCommentAdmin, self).log_change(request, object, message=message)

```

            for conn in self.connections:
                if hasattr(conn, "websocket") and conn.websocket:
>                   coros.append(conn.websocket.close_connection(after_handshake=False))
E                   TypeError: close_connection() got an unexpected keyword argument 'after_handshake'
```
As you can see from the `E` error here, conn.websocket.close_connection() doesn't expect an after_handshake arg.

issue_title: Problem with LocalProxy for EMAIL_SENDER
issue_body:
Flask-Security 3.0.0
Flask-Mail==0.9.1

The extension uses the attribute 'email_sender' for the mail sending operations, but the attribute is not a string, its a LocalProxy object:

    >>> current_app.extensions['security'].email_sender
    'no-reply@localhost'
    >>> type(current_app.extensions['security'].email_sender)
    <class 'werkzeug.local.LocalProxy'>

In core.py:

    'EMAIL_SENDER': LocalProxy(lambda: current_app.config.get(
        'MAIL_DEFAULT_SENDER', 'no-reply@localhost'
    )),

Which causes the call to 'send_mail' in utils.py (line 403) to fail because Flask-Mail calls 'sanitize_address' function as part of sending the msg:

    # flask_mail.py line 105
    def sanitize_address(addr, encoding='utf-8'):
        if isinstance(addr, string_types):
            addr = parseaddr(force_text(addr))

issue_title: calls to ldap.connection(username=..., password=...) are restrictive
issue_body:
Hey @etianen!

Thank you for all the work you have done on this library. I encountered an issue today when setting it up. The command `ldap_sync_users` calls `ldap.connection(username=...` making it so if you have a `username` Django **field** that has a different label than `username` it will produce an error in `utils.py L#38` when it tries to pop `username` from the `LDAP_AUTH_USER_FIELDS` and it doesn't find the key `username`.

In my instance my `username` is defined as `university_username` in my Django **Model**

I am trying to think of how this could be generalized, it would require that we either accept a command line argument that represents the `username` field in our model with the default fallback being `username` *or* we look into `LDAP_AUTH_USER_FIELDS` and take they keyword from there. The problem is I am not sure how we can always know which field would link to username, because what if the user model was based on `email` as the username or another field.

I guess the issue here is authenticating to ldap with a username and password correlates to the `LDAP_AUTH_USER_FIELDS` and it probably shouldn't have that dependency.

I think maybe the best thing to do would be provide an additional setting that you can apply that maps the ldap `username` to the model field

issue_title: Whys does DateField result in datetime column?
issue_body:
My model has a DateField, like so:

`timestamp = models.DateField()`

I create and run migrations, I end up with a `datetime` column rather than `date`. This has never been a problem since, somehow, Django is always given a python `date` for that field even though the column is datetime. e.g.

```
>>> SP.objects.all()[0].timestamp
datetime.date(2017, 5, 2)
```

I'm using unixodbc on Ubuntu 16. My driver is set to `/usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/odbc/libtdsodbc.so`

What's interesting is I was testing with Microsoft's Linux ODBC driver (set Driver to `/opt/microsoft/msodbcsql/lib64/libmsodbcsql-13.1.so.6.0`). If I do that and recreate my DB, then the migration creates a `date` column. (Note that the migration code is exactly the same)

To make this even more confusing, if I run the migration on Windows (so that neither unixodbc or MS's Linux odbc driver is involved) using MS's Windows ODBC Driver, I get a `datetime` column. So somehow, it's ok to have a `datetime` column for a DateField, it hasn't caused me problems until now.

Why does this all matter?

The problem is that I have database that was created via unixodbc. When I try to connect to that database from Linux using MS's Linux ODBC driver, the models returns a python datetime rather than date and this causes Exceptions in code that's already written and expects a python date. For example:

With unixodbc:
```
>>> SP.objects.all()[0].timestamp
datetime.date(2017, 5, 2)
```

Same exact database but accessed with MS's Linux driver:

```
>>> SP.objects.all()[0].timestamp
datetime.datetime(2017, 5, 2, 0, 0)
```

Any idea what is going on far beneath the surface?

https://github.com/williballenthin/python-evtx/blob/master/Evtx/Evtx.py#L232

https://docs.python.org/3.1/whatsnew/3.0.html#integers

The sys.maxint constant was removed, since there is no longer a limit to the value of integers. However, sys.maxsize can be used as an integer larger than any practical list or string index. It conforms to the implementation’s “natural” integer size and is typically the same as sys.maxint in previous releases on the same platform (assuming the same build options).

Thank you! Best regards!

issue_title: License audit - GPL2->LGPL2->CC0
issue_body:
While auditing the license changes that have occurred, we have come across some inconsistencies that need to be resolved.

- python-libarchive-c began life as https://github.com/dsoprea/PyEasyArchive, which was licensed GLP2 by the author of PyEasyArchive, dsoprea. The GPL2 license was added to python-libarchive-c by dsoprea here: https://github.com/Changaco/python-libarchive-c/commit/55235ba4472253886c905313573279dec009c429

- The GPL2 license was removed from python-libarchive-c in commit https://github.com/Changaco/python-libarchive-c/commit/7913bbadda8a99aed52881130c06aae4d4d40568 by Changaco, who was not the original author of the code, and was not authorised by the GPL2 to do so. At this point, python-libarchive-c was being distributed in violation of the GPL.

- This problem was brought to the attention of the python-libarchive-c project in the issue https://github.com/Changaco/python-libarchive-c/issues/7, and an attempt was made to solve the issue with https://github.com/Changaco/python-libarchive-c/pull/24, where Changaco switched licenses from the GPL2 to the LGPL2, and then immediately changing the licence to CC0.

- At the time of the license change, Changaco was not the copyright holder of the code, that was held by dsoprea. Section 2(b) of GPL2 states: "You must cause any work that you distribute or publish, that in whole or in part contains or is derived from the Program or any part thereof, to be licensed as a whole at no charge to all third parties under the terms of this License.". Given that Changaco's rights to the code were limited to those limited by GPL2, Changaco was not allowed to change the license to LGPL2, or to CC0. After https://github.com/Changaco/python-libarchive-c/commit/7913bbadda8a99aed52881130c06aae4d4d40568, any users of this library are no longer license compliant.

To fix this, the copyright holder https://github.com/dsoprea needs to authorise the license change made in https://github.com/Changaco/python-libarchive-c/commit/7913bbadda8a99aed52881130c06aae4d4d40568 and then in https://github.com/Changaco/python-libarchive-c/commit/2a6207dd36e095436290bcd528d55beaf77c724c.

issue_body:
It would be nice to have Infinite Scrolling built-in into Zinnia as an alternative to traditional pagination of entry lists.

* a settings option `ZINNIA_INFINITE_SCROLLING = (True|False)` could be introduced 
* the value of `ZINNIA_PAGINATION` should be re-used for the number of items to load initially and when reaching the bottom of the page

## Possible Solution Pieces

* Implementing a simple `ListView` returning a sliced result, e.g.
```
    start = page * settings.ZINNIA_PAGINATION
    entries = ...
    entries[start:start + settings.ZINNIA_PAGINATION]
```
* Using the unmaintained [infinite-scroll jQuery plugin](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/26600216/how-to-implement-infinite-scroll-endless-pagination-using-the-zinnia-blog-app-f).
* Using the [jScroll – jQuery Plugin for Infinite Scrolling](http://plugins.jquery.com/jscroll/).
* Using [django-endless-pagination](https://github.com/frankban/django-endless-pagination) and its [pagination on scroll](http://django-endless-pagination.readthedocs.org/en/latest/twitter_pagination.html#pagination-on-scroll).

issue_title: SuccessMessageMixin is useless with InlineViews
issue_body:
Using `forms_valid()` and never calling `form_valid()` means that the `SuccessMessageMixin` can't set any success messages. (`form_valid()` is where `SuccessMessageMixin` injects the messages)

To get round this, I made a new `InlineSuccessMessageMixin`

```python
from django.contrib import messages


class InlineSuccessMessageMixin(object):
    """
    Adds a success message on successful form submission.
    """
    success_message = ''

    def forms_valid(self, form, formset):
        print "something"
        response = super(InlineSuccessMessageMixin, self).forms_valid(form, formset)
        success_message = self.get_success_message(form.cleaned_data)
        if success_message:
            messages.success(self.request, success_message)
        return response

    def get_success_message(self, cleaned_data):
        return self.success_message % cleaned_data
```

It's basically just a copy of the django's built in one.

If you were to implement this, things to note:

It doesn't pass the formset forms' `cleaned_data` to the success message

issue_title: Incompatible to Django 2.0
issue_body:
In Django 2.0 foreign key relations must have the parameter `on_delete` set: https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/2.0/releases/2.0/#features-removed-in-2-0
This is not the case for the parent field of the `ThreadedComment` model. To reflect the behaviour in Django 1.11, it would have to set to models.DELETE

There are no error messages shown too for the printed output, but variant options doesn't appear in the Shopify store, only the product with 0 variants appears.

new_product = shopify.Product()
new_product.title = "My Product"
new_product.handle = "test-product"

##what I've tried... and countless others

new_product.variants = shopify.Variant({'options': {'option1' : ['S', 'M', 'L', 'XL'], 'option2' : ['Black', 'Blue', 'Green', 'Red']}, 'product_id': '123456789'})

                      {'title':'v1', 'option1': 'Red', 'option2': 'M'},
                      {'title':'v2', 'option1' :'Blue', 'option2' :'L'}
                      ])
new_product.save()
##No errors are output, but doesn't create variants with options
if new_product.errors:
    print new_product.errors.full_messages()
print "Done"
Thanks in advanced,

Chris

issue_title: [example/imagenet_classification] Continue training of pre-trained Inception-BN as prep step for finetuning
issue_body:
As exercise for fine-tuning I would like to continue training the inception-bn pretrained network on a subset of the imagenet 2012 training data using a new net with identical architecture to inception-bn (possibly later modifying learning rates in a subsequent step). Based on the example/imagenet_classification I have done the following steps:
1. Load the pre-trained model: loaded_model = mx.model.FeedForward.load(prefix = Inception-BN, load_epoch = 39, ctx = mx.gpu())
2. Load the network structure for the new net: import importlib, network = importlib.import_module('symbol_incpetion-bn').get_symbol(1000)
3. Store the model params of the pre-trained model: model_args = {'arg_params' : loaded_model.arg_params, 'aux_params': loaded_model.aux_params, 'begin_epoch' : load_epoch}
4. Create new model with arg_params and aux_params of the loaded model: model_new = mx.model.FeedForward( ctx = [gpu(0), gpu(1)], symbol = network, num_epoch = 41, learning_rate = 0.001, initializer = mx.init.Xavier(factor_type = "in", magnitude = 2.34, **model_args) 
5. Fit the model, here TrainIterator and ValIterator both contain the same subset from imagenet 2012 training data: model_new.fit(X = TrainIterator, eval_data = ValIterator, batch_end_callback = mx.callback.Speedometer(batch_size = 128, 50), eval_batch_end_callback = mx.callback.Speedometer(128, 50))

Contrary to my expectation of starting with good training and validation accuracy when doing the above train accuracy starts at 0.001, so around random.

I have manually checked my DataIterator to have correct labels (aligned to imagenet convention), for both TrainIterator and ValIterator, labels are also correct and in line with what I get when predicting from pre-trained inception-bn net.

Memory constantly increases until out of memory of machine.  Using the following code.  The memory does not release even after Mxnet finishes requested iterations.  If I use the gc() command, all memory is released.  **How can I call gc() during Mxnet optimizing?**  Or is there a different work around?

Input is a 16x16x3 data set.  Should be fairly easy to solve.  

`train_iter = mx.io.arrayiter(data = train.x, label = train.y    )`
`data <- mx.symbol.Variable('data')`

`tanh_1 <- mx.symbol.Activation(data= conv_1, act_type = "tanh")`
`fcl_2 <- mx.symbol.FullyConnected(data = tanh_1, num_hidden = 256)`
`fcl_2 <- mx.symbol.reshape(fcl_2, shape=c(16,16,1,128))`
`NN_Model <- mx.symbol.LinearRegressionOutput(data=fcl_2, name="lro")`

`conv.net.model <- mx.model.FeedForward.create(NN_Model, X=train_iter,`
`    ctx=mx.cpu(), num.round=150, array.batch.size=1,`

issue_title: Distributed and local train have different results when training a wide-deep network.
issue_body:
## Description
We see small but noticeable difference of outputs when we trained a wide-deep network. The difference only happened when we have both deep and wide parts of the network. We only used one worker and have the local command as  `OMP_NUM_THREADS=4 MXNET_CPU_WORKER_NTHREADS=24 python train.py some_other_params` and  distributed command as `OMP_NUM_THREADS=4 MXNET_CPU_WORKER_NTHREADS=24  incubator-mxnet/tools/launch.py  -n 1 --launcher=local python 
train.py some_other_params --kvstore dist_async`

To find out where the diff occurs, we have code like this to log the values/states
```
suffix = 'single' if args.kvstore is None else args.kvstore
mod.save_params('module-train-checkpoint-{}-{}.params'.format(suffix, nbatch))
mx.nd.save('batch_{}_{}_inputs.ndarray'.format(nbatch, suffix), batch.data)
mod.forward(batch)
outputs = mod.get_outputs()[0]
mx.nd.save('batch_{}_{}_outputs.ndarray'.format(nbatch, suffix), outputs)
```
**What we see is that both params and inputs are the same**, but the outputs from distributed and local train are different, which is really strange to me.

May I ask how to debug and check from here? Thanks.

issue_title: Duplication of Operators for sampling from random distributions
issue_body:
The operators defined in ``sample_op.cc`` and ``multisample_op.cc`` are seemingly performing the same tasks. Both these files define the following operators respectively
```
sample_op.cc
-----------
random_uniform
random_normal
random_gamma
random_exponential
random_poisson
random_negative_binomial
random_generalized_negative_binomial
```
```
multisample_op.cc
----------
sample_uniform
sample_normal
sample_gamma
sample_exponential
sample_poisson
sample_negative_binomial
sample_generalized_negative_binomial
```

The only difference that I can glean from the documentation is that operators in ``multisample_op.cc`` performs concurrent sampling from multiple distributions, but the behavior of the operators is not different.

Is ``sample_op.cc`` being retained for legacy reasons or backward compatibility? Can it be deprecated or EOLed? Correct me if I am wrong here.

issue_title: test_module.test_monitor has fixed seed that can mask flakiness
issue_body:
The unit test in title have been using fixed seed to mask flakiness. Suggested action:
1. Evaluate whether the test is flaky without fixed seed. If not, remove seed. Else move to 2
2. If test is flaky, determine whether it's an actual uncaught edge case. If so, fix the operator. Else move to 3
3. If numerical instability is inevitable, adjust tolerance level appropriately.

issue_title: Where can I find detailed documents about Executor class!
[scala] Accuracy precision is too low
issue_body:
## Description
When using the Accuracy EvalMetric, it is very easy to fall in cases where `EvalMetric.sumMetric` has a too low precision.

## Environment info (Required)
ubuntu, scala (not relevant).

## Build info (Required if built from source)
MXNet commit hash: 0449cc646a9f5d76e6c9fb01659e771bf1237233

## Description
In EvalMetric.scala, sumMetric is defined as:
```scala
  protected var sumMetric: Float = 0.0f
```
and the Accuracy metric does:
```scala
      for ((labelElem, predElem) <- label.toArray zip predLabel.toArray) {
        if (labelElem == predElem) {
          this.sumMetric += 1
        }
      }
```
Because sumMetric is a float, if it becomes bigger than ~10^7, then `this.sumMetric += 1` will have no effect and sumMetric will not be updated anymore. It is quite easy to get big numbers in sumMetric while solving, for example, image segmentation tasks (with output size of ~ 800x800, it will take about 50 output to get to 10^7). This won't throw any warning or error.

## Solutions
I worked around this issue by

1. summing all elements of one output before adding the result to sumMetric
2. dividing the sum by the number of elements

### 1) summing element before adding the result to sumMetric
To lower the difference of magnitude between this.sumMetric and 1, i replaced
```scala
      for ((labelElem, predElem) <- label.toArray zip predLabel.toArray) {
        if (labelElem == predElem) {
          this.sumMetric += 1
        }
      }
```
by
```scala
val ok = labe.toArrayl.zip(predLabel.toArray)
           .filter{ case (lElem: Float, pElem: Float) => lElem == pElem }.size.toFloat
this.sumMetric += s
```
s being (in my case) far bigger than 1, this change push back the problem. However, it doesn't solve it and that was not enough in my case.

### 2) Divide the sum by the number of elements
It would be better if the Accuracy doesn't depend on the size of the output. Hence, I divided the sum by the number of elements
 ```scala
val la = label.toArray
val size = label.size.toFloat
val ok = la.zip(predLabel.toArray)
            .filter{ case (lElem: Float, pElem: Float) => lElem == pElem }.size.toFloat
this.sumMetric += ok / size
```
With this change, I was able to train my network with about 50k inputs.

## Additional better (?) solution
We could use a `double` for this.sumMetric of EvalMetric, which would likely be enough for most case. The `get` function could still return a casted float if the EvalMetric API cannot easily be changed. 


I can provide a PR for the fix 1) and 2).
I am waiting for comments and feedbacks regarding the use of double for sumMetric.

My PR would also fix #8129 if @Ldpe2G (who found the solution) did not do it in the meantime. 

(By the way, am I the only one using Accuracy this way in Scala?)

#!/usr/bin/python
# filename: net.py

import mxnet as mx
from mxnet import nd, autograd, gluon
import numpy as np
mx.random.seed(1)


# running context
ctx_cpu = mx.cpu()

# get datasets
batch_size = 256
epoch = 10



def data_pre_deal(data, label):
    return nd.transpose(data.astype(np.float32),(2,0,1)), label.astype(np.float32)


cifar10_train = gluon.data.vision.CIFAR10('./cifar10/train', train=True, transform = data_pre_deal)
cifar10_test = gluon.data.vision.CIFAR10('./cifar10/test', train=False, transform = data_pre_deal)

# define data iterators
train_data = gluon.data.DataLoader(cifar10_train, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=True)
test_data = gluon.data.DataLoader(cifar10_test, shuffle=True, batch_size=batch_size)


# construction the bottleneck resnet unit
class bottleneck(gluon.HybridBlock):
    def __init__(self, filter_num, in_channels, stride=(1,1), prefix=None, params=None):
        super(bottleneck, self).__init__(prefix=prefix, params=params)
        self.stride = stride
        self.filter_num = filter_num
        self.in_channels = in_channels

        # create a sequential for residual
        self.res = gluon.nn.HybridSequential(prefix='')
        with self.res.name_scope():
            # add the first convolution layer
            self.res.add(gluon.nn.BatchNorm(axis=1))
            self.res.add(gluon.nn.Activation(activation='relu'))
            self.res.add(gluon.nn.Conv2D(channels=filter_num//4, in_channels=in_channels, kernel_size=1, strides=(1,1), layout='NCHW'))
            # add the second convolution layer
            self.res.add(gluon.nn.BatchNorm(axis=1))
            self.res.add(gluon.nn.Activation(activation='relu'))
            self.res.add(gluon.nn.Conv2D(channels=filter_num//4, in_channels=filter_num//4, kernel_size=3, strides=stride, padding=(1,1), layout='NCHW'))
            # add the third convolution layer
            self.res.add(gluon.nn.BatchNorm(axis=1))
            self.res.add(gluon.nn.Activation(activation='relu'))
            self.res.add(gluon.nn.Conv2D(channels=filter_num, in_channels=filter_num//4, kernel_size=1, strides=(1,1), layout='NCHW'))

            # create a sequential for shortcut
            if self.stride != (1,1) or self.filter_num != in_channels:
                self.shortcut = gluon.nn.HybridSequential(prefix='')
                self.shortcut.add(gluon.nn.Conv2D(channels=filter_num,in_channels=in_channels, kernel_size=1, strides=stride, layout='NCHW'))


    def hybrid_forward(self, F, x):
        res = self.res(x)
        if self.stride != (1,1) or self.filter_num != self.in_channels:
            sc = self.shortcut(x)
        else:
            sc = x

        out = sc + res
        return out


# construct the last few layers
class lastDense(gluon.HybridBlock):
    def __init__(self, units, prefix=None, params=None):
        super(lastDense, self).__init__(prefix=prefix, params=params)

        # create a sequential
        self.last = gluon.nn.HybridSequential(prefix='')
        with self.last.name_scope():
            self.last.add(gluon.nn.BatchNorm(axis=1))
            self.last.add(gluon.nn.Activation('relu'))
            self.last.add(gluon.nn.GlobalAvgPool2D(layout='NCHW'))
            self.last.add(gluon.nn.Dense(units))

    def hybrid_forward(self, F, x):
        return self.last(x)



# method to construct the model
resnet = gluon.nn.Sequential()
with resnet.name_scope():
    resnet.add(bottleneck(64, in_channels=3)) # 28x28x64
    resnet.add(bottleneck(128, in_channels=64, stride=(2,2))) # 14x14x128
    resnet.add(bottleneck(128, in_channels=128)) # 14x14x128
    resnet.add(bottleneck(256, in_channels=128, stride=(2,2))) # 7x7x256
    resnet.add(bottleneck(256, in_channels=256)) # 7x7x256
    resnet.add(lastDense(10))


# model initialization
resnet.collect_params().initialize(mx.init.Xavier(magnitude=2.24),ctx=ctx_cpu)

# definition of loss function and trainer
loss_fun = gluon.loss.SoftmaxCrossEntropyLoss()
trainer = gluon.Trainer(resnet.collect_params(), 'adam', {'learning_rate':1e-3, 'beta1':0.9, 'beta2':0.999, 'epsilon':1e-7})

# train the model
for e in range(epoch):
    for i, (data, label) in enumerate(train_data):
        data = data.as_in_context(ctx_cpu)
        label = label.as_in_context(ctx_cpu)
        with autograd.record():
            output = resnet(data)
            loss = loss_fun(output, label)
        print("epoch {}, iteration {}".format(e, i))

```

## Steps to reproduce
(Paste the commands you ran that produced the error.)

1. download the data cifar10 to the directory where the script stays into the directory cifar10/train and cifar10/test

2. run the script 
```
    $ python net.py
```

## What have you tried to solve it?
I have no idea why the memory allocated will not be freed in time. But if I do not use the 'self-defined block', there will be no such memory leakage problems.

issue_title: Distributed Training has inverse results when imported (8 GPUS is slower than 1!)
issue_body:
This issues will be demonstrated with 3 files
- *file 1*: distributed training in a single file
- *file 2*: a file which loads a net from another file and runs the same distributed learning
- *file 3*: the net from file 1 

There will also be 3 outputs
- *out 1*: file 1 run on 1, 2, 4, and 8 gpus
- *out 2*: file 2 running net from file 3 with 1 gpu
- *out 3*: file 2 running net from file 1 with 8 gpus

The important things to note here is that 1.) I modified the `get_mnist()` function to load from a local directory 2.) I am not running any evaluation metric in file 2 (but this is not factored into the run time) 3.)
using 8 gpus takes 18 seconds for file 2, but 2.7 for file 1, whereas it takes 2.3-3 seconds for 1 gpu in file 1 and 4 seconds for 1 gpu in file 2 (this makes no sense). 4.) the distributed training is an adaption of the chapter from the straight dope.

Lastly, one can call file 2 by something like:

`python3 distrubted.py -v -i test_net -g 1 -e 10`

where distributed.py is file2 and test_net.py is file 3

So what is going on?


# File 1
```
from __future__ import absolute_import, print_function, division

import mxnet as mx

from mxnet.context import Context
from mxnet.ndarray import array
