issue_title: Need support for complex numbers in our NI-FGEN and NI-SCOPE APIs
issue_body:
NI-FGEN and NI-SCOPE both support generating and acquiring complex waveforms. For example, NI-5541 and NI-5442 have Onboard Signal Processing that upconverts a complex waveform and generates it. The APIs use NIComplexI16 and NIComplexF64 to represent complex numbers.

nimi-python doesn't support those types.

issue_title: Simplify `nidcpower`, `nifgen` event configuration
issue_body:
[Zen of Python](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0020/#id3):

> Simple is better than complex.

NI-FGEN (and to a lesser degree NI-DCPower) provides a lot of configurability for triggers and events. You can change polarity, play with pulse width, set them as level instead of pulses... It's extremely rare to configure these and it's also not supported by the "latest and greatest" NI-FGEN device family 54x3.

For those reasons, we should remove the properties for all events:

* `event_level_active_level`
* `event_output_behavior`
* `event_pulse_polarity`
* `event_pulse_width`
* `event_pulse_width_units`
* `event_toggle_initial_state`
* `event_live_status`
* `event_latched_status`
* `event_delay`
* `event_delay_units`

Keep:

* `event_output_terminal`

For debate:

   * `nidcpower.Session.measure_complete_event_delay`:  Is there still a reason to keep this around?

issue_title: WebsocketClientProtocol: Expose HTTP status codes
issue_body:
Currently, if a server returns any status code that is not 101 the client dies silently, with no capability for the application to present this error information to the user.  At the very least, if the server returns 301 or 401 the application should have some way of detecting the failed handshake and presenting the user with "Authorization Required," rather than just exiting quietly.  Perhaps there could be a `onBadHTTPStatus()` function or something?

Most of the errors that cause a `failHandshake()` are serious errors, but HTTP status codes tend to be for errors that can be corrected.  Being able to display a 404 after a mistyped URL could be helpful, for example.

It seems like the only way to detect this now is to override the `failHandshake()` method and parse the error strings passed to it, which is clearly a rather brittle design.

issue_title: Add start and end tokens to source sentence in seq2seq DatasetReader
issue_body:
Two points here:
1) Is it intentional that you do not append the `@@END@@` token to the source sequence?
2) These `@@START@@` and `@@END@@` tokens are currently added inside the seq2seq dataset reader directly. However, there is also a way to add them via WordTokenizer params. Next, one could fetch them from the WordTokenizer instance inside the dataset reader for a future use in the decoder.

issue_title: Add caching for ELMo's uncontextualized word vectors
issue_body: From @matt-peters: We would like a caching strategy for ELMo where the uncontextualized word vectors are pre-computed for a fixed vocabulary. This speeds up computing the contextual vectors and reduces memory pressure.

issue_title: TextFields token_indexers keys are not used to set the vocabulary namespace
issue_body:
Issue:
The keys of the "token_indexers" dict passed to TextField aren't used to create the vocabulary namespace (I'm not entirely sure that this is the intended behavior but the tutorial on http://allennlp.org/tutorials/data-pipeline suggests so). Instead the TokenIndexers internal namespace is used, which leads to the behavior as shown in the example code. The specified namespace is "some_namespace" but the index is stored under the namespace "tokens", which is the default namespace for SingleIdTokenIndexer.

Example code:
```
from allennlp.data import Token
from allennlp.data.fields import TextField
from allennlp.data.token_indexers import SingleIdTokenIndexer
from allennlp.data import Instance
from allennlp.data import Dataset

from allennlp.data import Vocabulary

text_field = TextField(list(map(Token, ["Here", "are", "some", "longer", "words", "."])),
                       token_indexers={"some_namespace": SingleIdTokenIndexer()})
dataset = Dataset([Instance({"sentence": text_field})])

vocab = Vocabulary.from_dataset(dataset)
dataset.index_instances(vocab)

print('Namespaces:', vocab._index_to_token)
print('Namespace content:', vocab.get_index_to_token_vocabulary("some_namespace"))
```

Output:
```
Namespaces: defaultdict(None, {'tokens': {0: '@@PADDING@@', 1: '@@UNKNOWN@@', 2: 'Here', 3: 'are', 4: 'some', 5: 'longer', 6: 'words', 7: '.'}})
Namespace content: {0: '@@PADDING@@', 1: '@@UNKNOWN@@'}
```

issue_title: Command 'run' fails when using a non-standard SSH port
issue_body: When using a non-standard port, the deis run command fails with a "Connection refused" error. I tracked it down the fleet scheduler not taking SSH port into account while establishing an SSH connection. See https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/controller/scheduler/fleet.py#L261

issue_title: Nginx 5-tuple microflow affinity load balancing to enable arbitrary TCP
issue_body:
For arbitrary TCP connections, preserving affinity of packets in a

> 5-tuple {dest addr, source addr, protocol, dest port, source port}
> <cite>[RFC 6438](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6438#page-6)</cite>

commonly known as a

> Microflow: a single instance of an application-to-application flow of
> packets which is identified by source address, destination address,
> protocol id, and source port, destination port (where applicable).
> <cite>[RFC 2474](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2474#page-5)</cite>

is frequently expected of modern OSI Layer-4 load balancers, and is currently the default for [GCE](https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/load-balancing/network/target-pools#sessionaffinity) and [Azure](http://azure.microsoft.com/blog/2014/10/30/azure-load-balancer-new-distribution-mode/). As an OSI Layer-7 load balancer, I understand that Nginx terminates HTTP/1.0 "keep-alive" and HTTP/1.1 sessions by default. This can cause problems for mechanisms which take advantage of [Hop-by-hop](http://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2616#section-13.5.1) headers or analogues to perform a handshake over HTTP that results in the initiation of an alternate protocol. My understanding is that the termination of the HTTP session at the load balancer is the reason [the Nginx WebSockets module](http://nginx.org/en/docs/http/websocket.html) exists.

I hope someone more knowledgeable in the ways of Nginx would be willing to correct me if I misunderstand.

issue_title: Enable CoreOS rolling upgrades
issue_body: We've always [disabled](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/583f9f343dafa9df2fdfd1274f126e67b2fdf6cc/contrib/coreos/user-data.example#L25-L34) automatic updates on CoreOS, because we used to see incompatibilities between versions of etcd and fleet. Now that we're using the CoreOS stable channel and upgrades should be backwards-compatible, we should consider enabling updates as CoreOS does by default.

issue_title: best practice: Use separate volume for /var/lib/docker and local (fast) disk for etcd data directory
issue_body:
Using separate volumes for /var/lib/docker has improved etcd/fleet issues on Azure and EC2. Additionally, as of #2850, we are using the ephemeral drive on EC2 (which is an SSD) to store the etcd journal, which seems to have eliminated etcd/fleet timeout issues.

We should adopt this as a best practice for our provision scripts, wherever possible:

*Azure*
Will be using a separate `/var/lib/docker` volume once implemented in #2824. I believe the ephemeral disk on Azure is wiped on reboot, making it unusable for the etcd data directory.

*GCE*
We currently use a [separate volume](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/contrib/gce/gce-user-data-template) for Docker, but haven't yet investigated using local disks for the etcd journal.

*Rackspace and Digital Ocean*
No support for data disks? But using purely local disks may deem this change unnecessary.

*Bare metal*
Is this a performance issue on bare metal?

refs #2793
refs #2506
refs #2850

issue_title: Explore option of `globus` command starting an interactive REPL which uses ~/.globus_history
issue_body:
This is a bit of a wishlist feature.

One of the main `click` maintainers has a package which can do a basic REPL on arbitrary commands called [click-repl](https://github.com/click-contrib/click-repl).
However, this doesn't offer one of the important and attractive things about the REPL, which is the possibility of an isolated history. It also presents itself as a separate subcommand, whereas we might actually want it to be the default for our top-level command.
We do have some in-house experience writing our own captive shells, but that doubles as a cautionary tale -- we know how hard it is to get right.

One of the interesting notes about that implementation is that it maintains the same context object, which lets it act kind of like a "session". That means we could things like cache a user's bookmarks to avoid extra lookups, or store previously seen identity mappings and even tab complete them. It also exposes a rich featureset revolving around internal aliasing and variables.

In short, an interactive shell can be as complex as we find interesting and worthwhile.
However, this should not be an invitation to view this feature as necessary or by any means simple -- the number of questions it raises are significant.

issue_body:
### What is wrong?

According to some basic analysis the number of transactions in a block is responsible for most of the time it takes to process that block.

It would be interesting to see what kind of difference it makes to use the [batch writing API](https://plyvel.readthedocs.io/en/latest/user.html#write-batches) to reduce the number of times that we have to write directly to LevelDB.

### How can it be fixed

We could put a thin cache on top of the database within the database process, and have a periodic task that writes data from the cache into the database either when the cache reaches a certain size or after a set amount of time (ideally whichever comes first).

issue_title: Add function w3.eth.waitForTransactionReceipt(tx_hash)
issue_body:
* Version: 4

### What was wrong?

It's a bit cumbersome to wait for a transaction to be mined. For example, after deploying a transaction, example code typically involves a small while loop, like in #573 

This is a very common need, and should have an easy one-liner.

### How can it be fixed?

Something like:

```py
receipt = w3.eth.waitForTransactionReceipt(tx_hash)
contract = MyContract(receipt['contractAddress'])

# or

with w3.waitFor(lambda: w3.eth.getTransactionReceipt(tx_hash)) as receipt:
    contract = MyContract(receipt['contractAddress'])

```

Very open to alternative API suggestions.

This could potentially let us do cool things eventually, like use pubsub under the hood. Note that pubsub integration would be significantly easier in the first API than the second. But the first API implies adding other new functions that start with `waitFor` at some point in the future.

issue_title: Merging topic lists when creating filters not possible in current api.  Yields unexpected results.
issue_body:
### What was wrong?

The `contract.events.myEvent.createFilter` and `contract.eventFilter` both allow entering topics directly along with "argument filters" via `filter` param dict.  The implementation is built on a false assumption about the topic list.  The constructed topics are merged with the raw topics in a simple list addition:

```
    if topics is None:
        topic_set = construct_event_topic_set(event_abi, argument_filters)
    else:
        topic_set = [topics] + construct_event_topic_set(event_abi, argument_filters)
```

Merging two topic lists with additive results is impossible with a single filter.  Merging two topic lists by using the available OR logic within single topic positions would yield all topic combinations.

Example:
Given the following "logs": AA BB AB
Topic list 1 ["A", "A"] would match AA
Topic list 2 ["B", "B"] would match BB
merged list [["A", "B"], ["A","B"]] would match AA BB AB

### How can it be fixed?
Option 1. Remove option of entering raw topics along with argument filters that generate topics.
Option 2. Provide the functionality of merging topic lists, by extending the Filter object to manage multiple node filters.

something like:

```python
FilterAggregator(Filter):
    def __init__(self, web3, filter_ids):
        self.web3 = web3
        self.filter_ids = filter_ids
    
    def get_new_entries(self):
        log_entries = mapcat(
            compose(self._filter_valid_entries, self.web3.eth.getFilterChanges),
            filter_ids)
        return self._format_log_entries(log_entries)

    def get_all_entries(self):
            log_entries = mapcat(
                compose(self._filter_valid_entries, self.web3.eth.getFilterLogs),
                self.filter_ids)
        return self._format_log_entries(log_entries)
```

issue_title: Add distro as an optional dependency
issue_body: http://github.com/nir0s/distro is a new, more robust (and is now used by pip) implementation of Python's `platform.linux_distribution()` which will be removed in Python 3.7. We should first specify distro as an optional dependency for wagon (maybe allow to install it by running `pip install wagon[dist]`?) so that it can be used. We should keep using Python's implementation for now but deprecate it and then fully move to using distro (if still relevant) in Wagon v1.0.0

issue_title: UDF terminals are not modeled close enough
issue_body:
The current release leaves UDF (derivation) terminals as totally open-ended, which makes it difficult to convert to, say, the JSON serialization. While it's not documented well enough, Stephan has elaborated in an email that the possible forms are (probably):

* `(` form {id feature structure}+ `)`
* `(` form `)`
* `(` form start end `)`

The last form is probably only used by the LKB. Note that `start` and `end` are not characterization points, but inter-word positions (as with non-terminal nodes).

issue_title: Make conda have an option modify the path to the dynamic linker/loader
issue_body:
Much like
`conda --set-interpreter /path/to/ld-linux.so`

I've encountered a problem while using Red Hat 7.1 with Linuxbrew and Conda. Red Hat 7.1 is an old distribution and Linuxbrew provide a solution for having more up-to-date libraries (in special, glibc, dynamic linker/loader, gcc compiler, stdc++)  for those without root privileges (common case in clusters).

However, Anaconda distribution had all its binaries pointing to the old /lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2, which means that python packages compiled later via the gcc compiler provided by linuxbrew wouldn't work as conda python would load the old glibc but those compiled libraries would request incompatible but newer infrastructure libs (like libstdc++) provided by linuxbrew.

Here is the link to the problem I found and a solution: https://github.com/Linuxbrew/brew/issues/77#issuecomment-232248602

Just a suggestion. Not sure if it makes sense or just complicate things as the problematic python package came from pip, and not from conda repositories. But I suspect people might face very similar problems with many R packages that are not provided in the R channel.

issue_title: Add conda fetch or download command
issue_body:
As part of the work of creating offline environments I need a reliable way to retrieve a package and write it to another location.  The proposal is to add a `conda fetch` or `conda download` command.

## Proposed Interface

Note, `conda fetch` is used here, but it could be `conda fetch` or `conda download`

Here's an example that would simply download the packages and place the tarballs in the package cache.

```bash
$ conda fetch ipython-notebook
```

Here's an example that would download the packages but write them to another directory:

```bash
$ conda fetch ipython-notebook --directory=/path/to/dir
# or
$ conda fetch ipython-notebook -d /path/to/dir
```


## Proposed Implementation

This should work through the same mechanism as `conda install`.  It should build the index and determine what package to fetch / use.  If a local tarball is available in the package cache, fetch should not re-retrieve the file from the index.  If a local file is available and `-d|--directory` was used it should use that tarball when writing to disk.

issue_title: Package cache stomped over when using CONDA_SUBDIR
issue_body:
Based on the comments by Kale here:

https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/2014#issuecomment-300604221

You should be able to use the CONDA_SUBDIR environment variable to create 32-bit conda environments inside a 64-bit install. This seems to work, but has the problem that it pollutes the package cache with 32-bit packages! I tried alternating between creating 32-bit and 64-bit environments, and conda seems to be smart enough to avoid using the incorrect packages from the package cache, but since the package names don't specify bitness they get overwritten and conda has to redownload packages rather than pulling them from the cache.

CONDA_FORCE_32BIT seems to handle this better, by creating a "pkgs32" folder to cache the 32-bit packages separately. Although it sounds like that might be deprecated/removed soon. I could set CONDA_SUBDIR *and* CONDA_PKGS_DIRS when creating 32-bit environments, and unset them when creating 64-bit environments, but that seems prone to error. Are there plans to support subdir as more of a first class citizen for building packages and creating environments - e.g. allowing you to specify via the command line or in the meta.yaml/env.yaml, and keep the environments and packages separated by subdir? Or I am I better off using separate 32-bit and 64-bit coda installs?

issue_title: noarch python entry points created by conda differ than those created by conda-build
issue_body:
The entry point scripts which are created by conda for `noarch: python` packages differ slightly from those created for standard packages by conda-build.  The templates for the scripts are:

[conda](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda/gateways/disk/create.py#L37-L43):
``` python
python_entry_point_template = dals("""
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
if __name__ == '__main__':
    from sys import exit
    from %(module)s import %(func)s
    exit(%(func)s())
""")
```

[conda-build](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/blob/master/conda_build/utils.py#L61-L67):
``` python
PY_TMPL = """\
if __name__ == '__main__':
    import sys
    import %(module)s
    sys.exit(%(module)s.%(func)s())
"""
```
I've found at least one example where the template used by conda produces a script which does not work but when used with the conda-build template the script run fine.

issue_title: Fix TravisCI tests
issue_labels: [{'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda/labels/2+-+Someone+Needs+to+Work+on+This', 'name': '2 - Someone Needs to Work on This', 'color': 'CCCCCC'}, {'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda/labels/bug', 'name': 'bug', 'color': 'fc2929'}, {'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/conda/conda/labels/high+priority', 'name': 'high priority', 'color': 'ff0000'}]
issue_body: TravisCI tests are currently broken because Python 3.3 tests are not passing.  @asmeurer said he knew a way to fix this without a release of Miniconda.  We need to get this fixed as soon as possible so we can rely on TravisCI to produce accurate results instead of ignoring a series of failed tests.

issue_title: No miniconda3-latest
issue_body: There is a very useful `Miniconda-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh` in `http://repo.continuum.io/miniconda/` but not a corresponding file for Miniconda3. Having a link that doesn't change but always installs the latest version is very useful for deployments (e.g. in travis-ci or docker).

issue_title: Solver improvements: 2) translate hotspots functions in common.logic into compiled code
issue_body:
ref: https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/7676#issuecomment-415810372

> - Make `Clauses.BDD_` even faster by extracting it into a compiled extension module, i.e., compile `BDD_` and all methods that carry the `add_new_clauses` keyword argument.

This should be fairly easy. The most "difficult" thing here is just to decide into which language this should be transformed since `conda` up until now doesn't use compiled extensions directly.

Things to look out for/consider:
- We only have to care about the code paths in the logic functions (`Not_`, `And_`, ...) that use `add_new_clauses=True` for this, i.e., we can completely disregard that those functions can return tuples.
- Currently, inputs to all logic functions can be either integers or Booleans. It could make sense to translate `False`/`True` to some specific integers, e.g., `-sys.maxsize`/`sys.maxsize`, to avoid having to deal with different types here.
- This can be symbiotic with changing the clause storage, i.e., the implementation is likely simpler and faster if clauses are put into a flat integer array.

bgzf errors when using pysam.AlignmentFile.fetch in parallel (with multiprocessing)
issue_body:
I have a generator wrapping an iteration over a call to `pysam.AlignmentFile.fetch` in which I pass the `AlignedSegment` objects to a function that extracts information from them and yields this information:

```python
with pysam.AlignmentFile(args.bamfile, "rb") as bamfile:
    def generate_annotations():
        """Generates annotations for the alignments in *bamfile*."""
        for i, ali in enumerate(bamfile.fetch(), start=1):
            # [some code to display progress information]
            yield process_annotations(get_annotations(ali), ali)
```

I tried to "fork" this generator using `itertools.tee` in order to perform 4 different computations on the information extracted from the `AlignedSegment`, and then use each of the 4 resulting generator in a different process using `multiprocessing.Process` (synchronizing the processes with a lock in order to avoid too much memory usage by the tee). On a small test bam file, this seems to work.
However, on a larger bam file, this results in the following errors:

```
[E::bgzf_read] bgzf_read_block error -1 after 0 of 4 bytes
[E::inflate_block] inflate failed: invalid distance too far back
[E::bgzf_read_block] inflate_block error -1
[E::bgzf_read] bgzf_read_block error -1 after 0 of 4 bytes

[E::bgzf_read] bgzf_read_block error -1 after 0 of 4 bytes
[E::inflate_block] inflate failed: invalid distance too far back
[E::bgzf_read_block] inflate_block error -1
[E::bgzf_read] bgzf_read_block error -1 after 0 of 4 bytes
[E::bgzf_read] bgzf_read_block error -1 after 0 of 4 bytes
[E::inflate_block] inflate failed: invalid distance too far back
[E::bgzf_read_block] inflate_block error -1
[E::bgzf_read] bgzf_read_block error -1 after 0 of 4 bytes
[E::bgzf_read] bgzf_read_block error -1 after 0 of 4 bytes
[E::inflate_block] inflate failed: invalid distance too far back
[E::bgzf_read_block] inflate_block error -1
[E::bgzf_read] bgzf_read_block error -1 after 0 of 4 bytes

[E::inflate_block] inflate failed: invalid distance too far back

[E::bgzf_read] bgzf_read_block error -1 after 0 of 4 bytes
[E::bgzf_read] bgzf_read_block error -1 after 0 of 4 bytes
```

These errors are just printed, but do not seem to trigger an exception in python. If I understand correctly, this is because they are defined at the htslib C-code level (in file bgzf.c), and those errors are not treated as errors by pysam.

When I perform these computations in a pseudo-parallel approach (based on zipped accumulators as here: <http://stackoverflow.com/a/29509642/1878788>), I don't observe these error messages.



Note: I initially thought the problem had to do with pybedtools (<https://github.com/daler/pybedtools/issues/194>), but it seems that it is more a pysam issue.

issue_title: Make compile order deterministic on Python 3
GHDL : simulation options in wrong place on command line.
issue_body:
Using "set_sim_option" with ghdl's --elab-run command, the syntax expected by ghdl is that the elaboration options (e.g. "--std=08") are expected before the entity name, while the simulation runtime options (e.g. "--assert-level=none") must appear after it. 

However Vunit doesn't currently distinguish between elab and sim options, placing them both before the entity name on the command line.
Thus the following simulation options (elaboration options) work correctly, enabling code coverage via gcov (assuming ghdl built with the gcc back end)
```
vu.set_sim_option("ghdl.flags", ["-Wl,-lgcov"])
vu.set_sim_option("ghdl.flags", ["-Wl,--coverage"]) 
```
but the following simulation option (runtime option) does not:
`vu.set_sim_option("ghdl.flags", ["--assert-level=failure"])`
resulting in the message
"/usr/local/bin/ghdl: unknown option '--assert-level=failure' for command '--elab-run'

Command line usage of ghdl reveals:
```
ghdl --elab-run --std=08 --assert-level=none test
ghdl: unknown option '--assert-level=none' for command '--elab-run'

while
`ghdl --elab-run --std=08 test --assert-level=none`
works as expected.
```
See comment by Paebbels on https://github.com/tgingold/ghdl/issues/118 - apparently confirming this is a Vunit issue. I can work around the issue by changing the problematic asserts from severity Error (which abort the simulation) to severity Warning (which doesn't).

One approach would be to add a "set_elab_option" command with the current "set_sim_option" semantics (for building ghdl commands) and correct the "set_sim_option" semantics, another might be to distinguish between different kinds of "ghdl.flags".

Test case for the ghdl side on https://github.com/tgingold/ghdl/issues/118 : I'll make a Vunit testcase from it if that would help.

issue_title: Codec generation does not handle comments in types package
VUnit is not aware of the libraries added via the simulator config
issue_body:
The modelsim installation comes with pre compiled libraries (e.g. ieee, std, vital2000, etc) and it can also contain other pre compiled libraries (in my case all xilinx libs).

I don't like to use `add_external_library` from the VUnit api to make the simulator libraries known to VUnit, because then I don't like to duplicate the modelsim library administration by hand and synchronize all the paths on all build PCs.

When not using the `add_external_library`, VUnit prints a warning in the console because it cannot find the depending library. This is not a big deal but it adds a lot of confusing warning messages to the console.

I suggest that VUnit adds the known simulator libraries to the internal VUnit project. The `ModelSimInterface` already knows which simulator libraries are configured via the ini file. It seems that [ModelSimInterface.setup_library_mapping](https://github.com/VUnit/vunit/blob/master/vunit/modelsim_interface.py#L174) can be updated to add the simulator libraries to the internal VUnit project.

What do you think?

issue_title: Document "maxsize" (or equivalent) more prominently
issue_body:
I just had a scenario where I had to use Elasticsearch from a Tornado web service via its `ThreadPoolExecutor` integration. I was noticing terrible tail latencies on queries. After some digging, I discovered this was because of the `maxsize` parameter on the default connection class, [documented here](https://elasticsearch-py.readthedocs.org/en/master/connection.html#urllib3httpconnection-default-connection-class). The issue was that though my thread pool supported up to 500 threads, it was limited by the 10 concurrent connections that the driver had set on the underlying pool.

Thankfully, I could fix this by passing "maxsize" to the `Elasticsearch` object, since this then passes through to the connection object. But given how big an impact this parameter has on performance in multi-threaded scenarios, I think the documentation should call it out much more prominently. People are probably running very slow code in production without realizing it, even if they have big ES clusters, as a result of leaving this default.

issue_title: Clean up test failure output
issue_body:
Nose prints a ton of useless garbage when a test fails. One or two failing tests cause my CMD buffer to fill up with useless VCR output and actually prevents me from see the exception that caused the failure!

1) separate the HTTP requests and responses with other logs
2) group the HTTP requests and responses with corresponding command invocation.
3) Don't print the VCR debugging--or move it to a separate file. This is only useful if you want to figure out why requests are being matched (which is usually not the issue).

issue_title: [Command Consistency] Rules based Command Table analysis
issue_body:
Related to item #733. We already have a command table dump. It would be very useful if we analyzed the command table against certain rules that we want to enforce, or at least be alerted if there is an inconsistency. Some items might include:
- name type arguments without completers
- arguments in creates that have id_part=name and also have a completer
- arguments missing help
- arguments that use hard coded choice lists instead of reflecting on enums
- arguments or commands missing help text

This would help greatly as we get into the polish stage, relying on the rules to identify inconsistencies instead of the manual and error prone process of reviewing packages.

issue_title: aiohttp 2.x closing client request data stream
issue_body:
In previous versions of aiohttp, if you passed a stream as the `data` parameter to an aiohttp request, it would not close the stream after the request, now it is closing it because it's wrapping it in a `Payload`, and later the `write` method closes it.

I believe aiohttp should not be closing the stream sent to it. This new behavior caused this issue with aiobotocore: https://github.com/aio-libs/aiobotocore/issues/221 resulting in retries failing.

    """

    """
    keys = await get_logs(s3)
    r = await asyncio.gather(*[get_object(s3, k) for k in keys])

issue_title: Support for python 2.5
issue_body:

https://github.com/certifi/python-certifi/blob/38502797954603558ebf5f2c93f3645279e18158/setup.py#L45

But in same file it uses construction that is incompatible:
https://github.com/certifi/python-certifi/blob/38502797954603558ebf5f2c93f3645279e18158/setup.py#L10

issue_title: Python kernels crash when started from Docker with CMD without a shell
issue_body:
When the jupyter notebook is started as in this Dockerfile: https://gist.github.com/thewtex/baba0c786f8dcdc9c743
the Python kernel repeatedly crashes.

However, when started with a shell as here:
https://gist.github.com/thewtex/1316b3a2b4f3c170686b
the kernels do not crash.

If this is known and expected, it would be helpful to have a note in the "Known Issues" section of the documents, and have an example of what works in jupyter/notebook/Dockerfile.

issue_title: Feature request: Ability to log all input to a user defined logger (independent of kernel)
issue_body:
### Overview
As the title says I would like the ability to log all input cells using a user specified logging configuration file. The goal of this is twofold:

1. Have a logger that is kernel independent. I.e. Python, R and any other languages will be logged in the same manner.
2. Allow the user to specify how the input cells are logged. They can be sent to a file, database, server etc.

This can be done by adding an option at the command line to specify a logging configuration file with a specific logger name (kernel_logger). This file will be loaded in by notebook/services/kernels/handlers.py. All messages passed to the kernel with a code field will be logged to the kernel_logger.

### Implementation

I have implemented a version of this and will submit a pull request to the main repository.

issue_title: iopub_data_rate_limit
issue_body:
I ran into this error:

```
IOPub data rate exceeded.
The notebook server will temporarily stop sending output
to the client in order to avoid crashing it.
To change this limit, set the config variable
`--NotebookApp.iopub_data_rate_limit`.
```

While trying to run tfidfvectorizer like so:
```
lemmas = TIP_with_rats['s_lemmas_IP'].apply(lambda x: ' '.join(x))
vect = sklearn.feature_extraction.text.TfidfVectorizer()
features = vect.fit_transform(lemmas)
feature_names = vect.get_feature_names()
dense = features.todense()
denselist = dense.tolist()
print(denselist)
```

And after a minute of googling found that the solution seems to be to start jupyter with `jupyter notebook --NotebookApp.iopub_data_rate_limit=10000000`

However when I do that using Anaconda Prompt it opens a different jupyter window with a different list of files (it looks like system files, stuff with a .exe extension). This My existing Jupyter notebook and Jupyter lab don't register the increased data rate limit and Anaconda Prompt returns permissions errors if I try to open any of the files, add anything new, or upload existing jupyter notebooks.

I can't seem to find any documentation on this - any ideas on how to fix?

issue_title: More powerful image uploader/editor
issue_body:
We should create an image uploader that can crop and apply simple filters to images in the image archive. I propose to upload images just like we do today (we may want to enhance the UI a bit), and then apply cropping and filtering after the image has been uploaded. issue_title: BEM block for form element wrapper
issue_body:
We currently have good but a bit limited form styling where we assume each field is just a label with an input/textarea.

We need to be able to add the following related to each field:
- help text - can be more than one "paragraph". This is already in ``cradmin_theme_full/4__components/generalpurpose/_formfield-help-text.scss``, but that should probably be removed in favor of this new structure.
- zero or more warning messages. May want to just use a variant of #66.
- zero or more success messages. May want to just use a variant of #66.

We may also want to be able to give the wrapper the following variants:
- success
- warning

I have added a structure draft/example:

issue_title: Implement software_agent token generation
issue_body:
The software_agent functionality is live at https://dev.dataservice.duke.edu/apiexplorer.
What this boils down to is there is a new endpoint ```POST /api/v1/software_agents/api_token``` that will return a auth token for use with all the other endpoints. This endpoint doesn't require authentication.
It requires two keys:
- User API key: ```GET /api/v1/current_user/api_key```
- Software Agent API key: ```GET /api/v1/software_agents/{id}/api_key``` where id is the uuid for our software agent.

**The troublesome part is the token is only good for two hours.** So a really long download or upload will fail with a 401 after that time.  We could try to get this extended, but given a big enough file we will run into this issue.

Here's the actual loss, `bk == autograd.numpy` here, `data` and `theta` are `namedtuple` (the packing-unpacking part works just fine with `autograd` here, tested with other losses):

    def predict(self, theta, data):

            hidden2hidden = bk.dot(h_prev, theta.W_hidden_to_hidden)

issue_body:
There is a  bug similar to #148 where a zip file can be missing directory entries if created by Windows.

To reproduce this error,  I extracted the files in the syslog.zip file found in the test_data directory, placed the files in a folder named `folder` and then (in Windows 7) right clicked on the folder and used the 7z context menu to create `syslog_7z.zip` and the *"Sent To -> Compressed (zipped) folder"* menu to create `syslog_win.zip`.

Then running the following..

```python
#Necessary to prevent circular import.
from dfvfs import resolver

from dfvfs.path import zip_path_spec, os_path_spec
from dfvfs.vfs import zip_file_system
from dfvfs.resolver import context

for path in ['syslog_7z.zip', 'syslog_win.zip']:
  path_spec = os_path_spec.OSPathSpec(location=path)
  path_spec = zip_path_spec.ZipPathSpec(location'/', parent=path_spec)

  file_system = zip_file_system.ZipFileSystem(context.Context())
  file_system.Open(path_spec)

  file_entry = file_system.GetFileEntryByPathSpec(path_spec)
  
  print '==='
  print path_spec.comparable
  for sub_file_entry in file_entry.sub_file_entries:
    print sub_file_entry.path_spec.comparable
    for sub_sub_file_entry in sub_file_entry.sub_file_entries:
      print sub_sub_file_entry.path_spec.comparable
```
produces
```
===
type: OS, location: D:\syslog_7z.zip
type: ZIP, location: /

type: OS, location: D:\syslog_7z.zip
type: ZIP, location: /folder/

type: OS, location: D:\syslog_7z.zip
type: ZIP, location: /folder/syslog

type: OS, location: D:\syslog_7z.zip
type: ZIP, location: /folder/wtmp.1

===

```

As you can see, the it fails to find the files in the Windows version. 

The solution for #148 should also work for this.

issue_title: Handle backward compatibility
issue_body:
NailGun generally tries to keep up with new versions of Satellite as they are released. This is great for developers and those running the current version of Sat, but it's terrible for anyone running an older version of Satellite. It would be great if there was some mechanism by which we could check to make sure that NailGun is not breaking compatibility with older versions of Satellite. This is already somewhat feasible: NailGun has the ability to store a version in its `ServerConfig` objects, and those version numbers are used to conditionally execute several work-arounds.

If each new release of NailGun breaks compatibility with old versions of Satellite, then release numbers are essentially meaningless. We should either come up with some system for checking for backward compatibility, or we should come up with a statement saying which versions are supported by a given NailGun version.

issue_title: Question about the "right" way for writing plugins
issue_body:
I started looking into pluggy more and thought about how to use it right from the perspective of a plugin writer. I looked through the docs and came to the conclusion that the right way of doing stuff is to use the pluggy API to create a hookimpl object instead of importing it from the host project. I started changing the tox code and opened a whole slew of PRs to change to the "right" way. Long story short - I might have been a bit trigger happy and now I would like to clarify this and then either go ahead or undo the damage. My opinion at the moment is that using the pluggy API explicitly is the right way rather than importing the hookimpl from the host. In tox we already have the problem of several hookimpl objects that are imported by plugins. So I am talking about

    import pluggy
    hookimpl = pluggy.HookImplMarker("project")

vs

    from project.where.ever.it.lives import hookimpl

@ryanhiebert argues that the plugin writer doesn't have to care and should not have to do this and instead get the hookimpl marker directly from the host. I have to admit that this makes a lot of sense, but atm I am still torn, so I would be interested what the official view of the core devs is. Reading the docs again with that perspective in mind it seems clear that hookimpl should be imported. Also taking this further means that the underlying plugin implementation could be replaced as long as the marker object, decorator args and passed in objects stay the same, so importing the object might be the better idea from that perspective also.
 
Once this is clarified one or the other way I would volunteer to enhance the docs to make it clearer how this should be done and why.

Also cc @asottile who already started using that approach in another project that is not in tox-dev.

affected PRs:

https://github.com/tox-dev/detox/pull/12
https://github.com/tox-dev/tox-durations/pull/3
https://github.com/tox-dev/tox-pipenv/pull/40
https://github.com/tox-dev/tox-travis/pull/103

issue_title: Add note to docs about pytest-cov and pytest doctest support
issue_body:
A coworker of mine covinced himself that the built-in pytest doctest support module didn't integrate with pytest-cov. He wrote his shim to "get around" this "limitation" but I think he was just using the command-line arguments wrong!

Seems like the gist is just to make sure you have "--doctest-modules" and "--cov-report" and "--cov" arguments, in your command (or config file).

like ...

```bash
py.test --doctest-modules . --cov-report term --cov=.
```

or

```bash
py.test --doctest-modules mymodule --cov-report term --cov=mymodule
```

I think his issue was that he "couldn't find any documentation" to tell him just what to do. I guess he didn't think to just try the arguments all together. I think he was forgetting `--cov` argument and confusing himself.

So maybe it would be good to just include a usage example like the above, in some docs somewhere. When you look for "doctest" in the pytest-cov docs you don't find anything. Maybe this little FAQ or footnote should be stuffed somewhere.

Happy to make a PR... just unclear where to stick this.

issue_title: Correction/Improvement for DownloadFileMixin
issue_body:
I have used the following FileMixin code to test some file downloads from ASM endpoint:

https://f5-common-python.slack.com/files/wojtek0806/F2C2XAD1V/mixins.py

a = mr.tm.asm.file_transfer.downloads.download_file('C:\exported_file.xml', 
                                                    chunk_size=100)

Regrettably the resulting file is 0 bytes long, when debugging I have found that during first while loop iteration we set the content-size to following: '0-99/0' , this would be fine, however because of the following code:

````
# Determine the total number of bytes to read.
                if size == 0:
                    size = int(crange.split('/')[-1]) - 1

                    # If the file is smaller than the chunk_size, the BigIP
                    # will return an HTTP 400. Adjust the chunk_size down to
                    # the total file size...
                    if chunk_size > size:
                        end = size

                    # ...and pass on the rest of the code.
                    continue
````

We update content-size to following:  '0--1/-1', this means that when we reach up to this part of code:
````
           if size > 0:
                        current_bytes += chunk_size
                        for chunk in response.iter_content(chunk_size):
                            writefh.write(chunk)
````
we never execute and when we hit:
```
                if end == size:
                    break
```
We exit loop and writing to file finishes.

I have tried to fix this with the following: 

https://f5-common-python.slack.com/files/wojtek0806/F2C34G021/mixins_py.py

This will work, if you do not specify the chunk_size:

issue_title: Product attribute's code generated with dashes
issue_body:
Let's see in the following code:
```python
>>> class Tmp(object):
...     pass
...     
>>> tmp = Tmp()
>>> setattr(tmp, 'temp-code', 123)
>>> tmp.temp-code
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<input>", line 1, in <module>
AttributeError: 'Tmp' object has no attribute 'temp'
>>> tmp.__dict__
{'temp-code': 123}
>>> getattr(tmp, 'temp-code')
123
```
Attribute was set, object have this attribute, but this attribute can not be accessed directly as `tmp.temp-code`. That's right, this is Python.
Product's attributes with spaces in their names have generated code with dashes. So, these attributes can not be accessed with `product.attr.some-attribute`, because of dash in attribute's codename is interpreted as minus sign in program's code. Documentation for `ProductAttributesContainer` offer this way to get attribute's values, but one way to get value is `getattr(product.attr, 'some-attribute')`.
This behavior can be fixed with using AutoSlugField with underscore as separator [here](https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/master/src%2Foscar%2Fapps%2Fcatalogue%2Fabstract_models.py#L780-L785):
```python
class AbstractProductAttribute(models.Model):
    ...
    code = models.SlugField(
        _('Code'), max_length=128,
        validators=[RegexValidator(
            regex=r'^[a-zA-Z\-_][0-9a-zA-Z\-_]*$',
            message=_("Code can only contain the letters a-z, A-Z, digits, "
                      "minus and underscores, and can't start with a digit"))])
    ...
```
Any thoughts?

issue_title: Rename RID with alpine in name
issue_body:
Sabotage Linux - http://sabotage.tech/
Void Linux - https://www.voidlinux.eu/
NodeOS - http://node-os.com/

are some examples of musl-libc based linux distributions. Here is a full list: https://wiki.musl-libc.org/projects-using-musl.html.

Currently `microsoft/dotnet:2.1-sdk-alpine` docker image has hardcoded alpine name in it and unlike `dotnet-sdk-latest-linux-x64.tar.gz` distro-agnostic package, there is no way of using alpine package on any-musl distro.

Please consider renaming alpine docker and tarball to have musl in its name instead of alpine, so other distros based on this libc can get the (non-official) support.

Example of using .NET Core on some non-officially supported glibc-based distros:

* Mageia Linux - https://gist.github.com/anonymous/1b45b87032d62bf20bf28ad7512ea178 (listed on top 10 on distro watch https://www.distrowatch.com/dwres.php?resource=major)
* Clear Linux - https://github.com/dotnet/coreclr/issues/9055#issuecomment-371758941

CC @janvorli @MichaelSimons

issue_title: Handle exceptions and interrupts for storage.py
issue_body: It is possible that an exception or signal could interrupt storage.py while it is modifying a lineage, leaving the associated files in an invalid state. Such critical sections of code should be identified and recovery methods should be written to attempt revert the lineage to a valid state before program exit.

issue_title: Webroot breaks for non-root due to chown
issue_body:
Hi,

Today I'm trying out letsencrypt and stumbled upon that using webroot breaks when not using root due to a chown on line 72 of letsencrypt/plugins/webroot.py:

```
 49     def prepare(self):  # pylint: disable=missing-docstring
 50         path_map = self.conf("map")
 51
 52         if not path_map:
 53             raise errors.PluginError("--{0} must be set".format(
 54                 self.option_name("path")))
 55         for name, path in path_map.items():
 56             if not os.path.isdir(path):
 57                 raise errors.PluginError(path + " does not exist or is not a directory")
 58             self.full_roots[name] = os.path.join(path, challenges.HTTP01.URI_ROOT_PATH)
 59
 60             logger.debug("Creating root challenges validation dir at %s",
 61                          self.full_roots[name])
 62             try:
 63                 os.makedirs(self.full_roots[name])
 64                 # Set permissions as parent directory (GH #1389)
 65                 # We don't use the parameters in makedirs because it
 66                 # may not always work
 67                 # https://stackoverflow.com/questions/5231901/permission-problems-when-creating-a-dir-with-os-makedirs-python
 68                 stat_path = os.stat(path)
 69                 filemode = stat.S_IMODE(stat_path.st_mode)
 70                 os.chmod(self.full_roots[name], filemode)
 71                 # Set owner and group, too
 72                 os.chown(self.full_roots[name], stat_path.st_uid,
 73                           stat_path.st_gid)
 74
 75             except OSError as exception:
 76                 if exception.errno != errno.EEXIST:
 77                     raise errors.PluginError(
 78                         "Couldn't create root for {0} http-01 "
 79                         "challenge responses: {1}", name, exception)
```

It tries to set the ownership of the directory to the owner of the parent. So currently to bypass this issue either the webroot must be chown'd to the letsencrypt user, or the script needs to be run as root. Neither of which are satisfactory solutions. Is chown at all neccesary when letsencrypt is not run as root?

issue_title: Remove unnecessary subclasses
issue_body:
With 2.0, the only reason to have a subclass would be to create syntactic sugar for things that aren't super easy to do with the raw API. One example is the formatting of email addresses for the transmissions API. For API endpoints that we don't need sugar for, we should remove the subclasses.

* Metrics
* Recipient Lists
* Suppression List
* Templates

issue_title: Allow comma as a separator between seconds and microseconds
issue_body:
According to http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_8601#Times (relevant quote: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_8601#cite_note-15) the preferred decimal sign should be a comma (although a dot is also valid).

Currently a comma is not recognised (but a dot is).

````
>>> parse("2015-02-05T13:34:04,000+0000")
[...]
ValueError: unknown string format

>>> parse("2015-02-05T13:34:04.000+0000")
datetime.datetime(2015, 2, 5, 13, 34, 4, tzinfo=tzutc())
```

issue_title: @override_settings doesn't play nicely with SALESFORCE_DB_ALIAS setting
issue_body:
In attempt to balance both completeness and speed, I have some tests which use a local datastore and some which reach out to Salesforce. I try to keep the Salesforce tests small -- just enough to test all of the required core functionality -- and then rely on the local datastore for testing more complex behavior. (This also helps with API usage limits.)

The natural way to do this is would seem to be with the `@override_settings(SALESFORCE_DB_ALIAS='')` decorator -- use this to set the salesforce db to the remote datastore *only* for connectivity tests, and leave it set to a local datastore the rest of the time.

I was coming up against a strange bug where column Ids weren't being set correctly. After some thinking and sleuthing, I found that at least part of the problem lies with how database alias-checking is done in django-salesforce: there are places where the alias is checked on load, and then never again (ie live changes won't affect it). Thus, any updating of the `settings` isn't going to be reflected (I assume this is copy-by-value).

I'll make up a pull request to demonstrate what I mean by this.

issue_body:
When I trying to add a FastMarkerCluster() layer to my multilayer map, the layer selector in the top right of the browser window disappears.

#### Code Sample:

```python
import pandas as pd
import folium

df = pd.read_csv(r'raw_data.csv')
data = df[['lat', 'long']].values.tolist()

m = folium.Map() 
folium.plugins.HeatMap(data, name="layer1").add_to(m)
folium.plugins.HeatMap(data, name="layer2").add_to(m)
#folium.plugins.FastMarkerCluster(data, name="layer3").add_to(m)

folium.LayerControl().add_to(m)
m.save("map.html")

```
#### Problem description

When I run the above as is, I get a layer selector in the top right which I can use to switch on/off the two heatmap layers. When I uncomment the FastMarkerCluster() line and run the code, the cluster layer is added to the map, however the layer selector is no longer present.

#### Expected Output

I expect the layer selector in the top right corner of the browser window to be present when adding layers using FastMarkerCluster()

issue_title: Allow field.attribute for both serializing and deserializing
issue_body:
I'm happy to take a stab at coding this but wanted to get a sense if it would be a useful feature for others, or if there is way to handle this in the current tooling that I am missing.  

I have to consume a number of .NET created apis which provide their keys in PascalCase.  As a result I need to specify how attributes would map a lot.  It would be great it Marshmallow allowed you specify something like field.attribute to be use for serialization and one to be used for deserialization.  For example:

```
class User(object):
    def __init__(self, name, email, age=None):
        self.name = name
        self.email = email

class UserSchema(Schema):
    name = fields.String(deserialize_from="Name")
    email_addr = fields.String(attribute="email", deserialize_from="EmailAddress")

user = User('Keith', email='keith@stones.com')
ser = UserSchema()
result, errors = ser.dump(user)
pprint(result)
# {'name': 'Keith',
#  'email_addr': 'keith@stones.com'}

payload = {
                   'Name': 'Keith',
                   'EmailAddress': ''keith@stones.com',
}

ser = UserSchema()
result, errors = ser.load(payload)
pprint(result.data)
# {'name': 'Keith',
# 'email': ''keith@stones.com'}
```

Maybe make `deserialize_from` be an additional search key that way you could still deserialize a previously serialized instance.

Any interest in this idea?

issue_title: Python DataSet of ImageFrame needs to support predict, evaluation or have API to get ImageFrame

Either provide APIs to do prediction/evaluation, or provide API to get ImageFrame from Dataset, then use imageFrame to do prediction/evaluation. Also, ImageFrame does not have API to do evaluation, we may need to provide this API or provide API to extract Sample RDD from imageframe to do evaluation.

issue_title: Incorrect result for (^ x 0.0) with positive x
issue_body:
The following input returns `unsat` with Z3 4.4.1, but `sat` with master:
```
(declare-fun x () Real)
(declare-fun y () Real)
(assert (> x 0.0))
(assert (= y (^ x 0.0)))
(assert (distinct y 1.0))
(check-sat)
```

The model should be `unsat`, given the fact that `x` is positive.  This regression was introduced with this bugfix https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/commit/035f2bb0dae81519e550a9c865c2aab93eac2dc2.  The issue is that the y.is_zero() case in purify_arith_tactic::process_power is currently only applied if `(y.is_zero() && complete())`, which causes a fall-through in the case that `y.is_zero()` is true but `complete()` is false.

Based on the way a similar problem with even root handling was changed in https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/commit/ee0dbf34f0d1bbded0512aef5168ea8c92e30df0, my guess is that the fix for should look like this: https://github.com/dstaple/z3/commit/87b767424582b08b7df6814e12c5d96e29227164.  If this is correct, let me know and I'll put in a pull request and submit a regression test.

issue_title: Z3 picks control characters in strings - e.g. '\x00'
issue_body:
It appears that Z3 is free to pick any 8-bit character value for characters in a string, and defaults to picking '\0' when it is able to:

>>> a = String("a")
>>> s = Solver()
>>> s.add(Length(a) == 5)
>>> s.check()
sat
>>> s.model()
[a = "\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00"]

This seems incorrect, as a string of 5 NULL characters is just the empty string. 

I have tried adding additional constraints of the form:
Not(Contains(string, StringVal('\\x00')))

This has two issues:
- returns unknown if no restriction is placed on length
>>> s.add(Not(Contains(a, "\\x00")))
>>> s.check()
unknown
- The set of characters that I wish to exclude is large (0-31; 128-255), and this slows down the solution generation for Strings considerably (~5x).

Is it possible to make a change to Z3 itself to prevent it from picking these control/special characters? If this is not considered to be useful more widely I can make the change myself if given a pointer to what changes are needed. 

Thanks,
Chris

issue_title: Eliminate tools specific settings
issue_body:
https://github.com/0xc0170/project_generator/commit/f204880856437704d507950fa5a4d10ba95bf58e

Somewhere around this commit we used to have a minimal set of options for the yaml files. Then things changed and there are specific options for specific tools and I dont think this is the right approach. I think we should define what the yaml files are capable of and then let the scripts figure out the best setting when generating a project file rather than having the yaml creater also be intimately familar with the toolset. Otherwise I fear a feature like https://github.com/0xc0170/project_generator/issues/51 will never be possible.

I checked the source code of explore in pathgroup.

```
    def explore(self, stash=None, n=None, find=None, avoid=None, find_stash='found', avoid_stash='avoid', cfg=None, num_find=1, step_func=None):
        """
        Tick stash "stash" forward (up to "n" times or until "num_find" paths are found), looking for condition "find",
        avoiding condition "avoid". Stores found paths into "find_stash' and avoided paths into "avoid_stash".

        The "find" and "avoid" parameters may be any of:

        - An address to find
        - A set or list of addresses to find
        - A function that takes a path and returns whether or not it matches.

        If an angr CFG is passed in as the "cfg" parameter and "find" is either a number or a list or a set, then
        any paths which cannot possibly reach a success state without going through a failure state will be
        preemptively avoided.
        """
        num_find += len(self.stashes[find_stash]) if find_stash in self.stashes else 0
        tech = exploration_techniques.Explorer(find=find,
                                   avoid=avoid,
                                   find_stash=find_stash,
                                   avoid_stash=avoid_stash,
                                   cfg=cfg,
                                   num_find=num_find)
        self.use_technique(tech)
        out = self.run(stash=stash,
                       step_func=step_func,
                       n=n)
        out.remove_tech(tech)
        self.remove_tech(tech)
        return out
```

I find it uses _angr.exploration_techniques.explorer.Explorer_,but I want to use _angr.exploration_techniques.looplimiter.LoopLimiter_ at the same time. How can I make it?

issue_title: Satisfiability bug with CompositeFrontend
issue_body:
Hello !

I have found a sequence of step that makes the CompositeFrontend return invalid satisfiability.

I will just drop you the script to reproduce the issue :

```
import claripy

full_frontend = claripy.FullFrontend(claripy.backends.z3)
s =  claripy.CompositeFrontend(full_frontend)

#s = claripy.Solver()

c = claripy.BVS('test', 32)
s.add(c[7:0] != 0)

print(s.satisfiable())

s.add(c == 0)

s.satisfiable(extra_constraints=[claripy.BVS('lol', 32)==0])

print(s.satisfiable())
```

You can see that at the end, the constraints are clearly not satisfiable (c must be null but not null), but my satisfiable() call with extra_constraints containing another variable seems to confuse the CompositeFrontend.

If you remove that satisfiable(extra_constraints=...), or if you use the default claripy.Solver(), the bug will not appear.

Thanks :)

issue_title: LocationGlobal should require an altitude value
issue_body:
It is possible to declare LocationGlobal without specifying an alt. This then fails at runtime when you try use the variable with `struct.error: required argument is not a float`:
```
lat_value=10
lon_value=20
new_location = LocationGlobal(lat_value, lon_value) 
vehicle.home_location = new_location
```
This arises from https://github.com/dronekit/dronekit-python/issues/514#issuecomment-172996320

I think this is a bug. Ideally the calling method(s) should check that the locationglobal (or other location) is valid. I suspect this defaults to `None` but the float warning is unhelpful.

issue_title: django.db.migrations.loader.BadMigrationError: Migrated app 'menu' contains South migrations.
issue_body:
1. django.db.migrations.loader.BadMigrationError: Migrated app 'menu' contains South migrations. Make sure all numbered South migrations are deleted prior to creating Django migrations.

2. django.db.migrations.loader.BadMigrationError: Migrated app 'dashboard' contains South migrations. Make sure all numbered South migrations are deleted prior to creating Django migrations.

The solution is renaming the *migrations* package in *south_migrations* 

At the moment I have fixed it indicating an other 'migrations' package for them putting this code in my settings.py:

SOUTH_MIGRATION_MODULES = {
    'cms': 'cms.migrations',
    ...
    
    #FIX BAD MIGRATION ERRORS THROUGH TEMPORARY WORKAROUND
    'menu': 'cms.migrations',
    'dashboard': 'cms.migrations',
    'django_extensions': 'cms.migrations',
    'filer': 'cms.migrations',

  ...
}

The same problem for django_extensions, but it has been resolved but not pushed to pypi(as you can read [here](https://github.com/django-extensions/django-extensions/issues/760) ), and for django_filer (as you can read [here](https://github.com/stefanfoulis/django-filer/issues/603) )

issue_title: Rewriting imported documents (<link rel="import">). Chrome only currently
issue_body:
Chrome includes an implementation of HTML imports ([draft spec](https://w3c.github.io/webcomponents/spec/imports)) which allows for a document to include and reuse HTML documents in other HTML documents. 
This means that the importing document (known as the **import  referrer** and **master document**) from one domain into the current browser context.
Because [imported documents don't have browsing context](https://w3c.github.io/webcomponents/spec/imports/#h-issue), scripts included in the imported document are executed in the **master documents** context.

When replaying or recording [YouTube](https://www.youtube.com/) using Chrome, the service worker normally registered using https://www.youtube.com/sw.js is actually registered using https://s.ytimg.com/sw.js.
This happens because YouTube is serving a version of itself to Chrome that uses HTML imports 
```html
<link rel="import" href="https://s.ytimg.com/yts/htmlbin/desktop_polymer_sel_auto_svg_home-vfl8KfIUN.html" name="desktop_polymer_sel_auto_svg_home"> 
```
and `wbinfo` states that the browsing context is `https://s.ytimg.com` not `https://www.youtube.com`.
See also issue #322  and the discussion in PR #323. 

YouTube also adds (via JS) `link rel=preload` tag to prep for another `link rel=import` containing  JavaScript that manipulates browser history.
Because the current `wombat` rewriting configuration is setup  to only adds the `cs_` modifier to `link href="URLs"`    
```html
<!-- lw -->
<link as="document" rel="preload" href="https://s.ytimg.com/yts/htmlbin/desktop_polymer_sel_auto_svg_home_deferred-vfleF3EET.html">
<!-- rewritten -->
<link as="document" rel="preload" href="http://localhost:8080/live/cs_/https://s.ytimg.com/yts/htmlbin/desktop_polymer_sel_auto_svg_home_deferred-vfleF3EET.html">
```

Links clicked that normally should be displayed in the navigation bar as `http://localhost:8080/live/https://www.youtube.com/feed/trending` become `http://localhost:8080/live/cs_/https://www.youtube.com/feed/trending`. 
This is again due to the usage of HTML imports which is Chrome specific.
For reference, when viewed using FireFox (not affected) the HTML import behavior is emulated by adding a `div` to the `head` of the **master document** (`https://youtube.com`).

To fix this issue in wombat, when using Chrome, an investigation into the using `document.currentScript.ownerDocument.defaultView` to validate the information embedded in `wbinfo` may prove usefull.
Also the Chrome devtools console reports that the browser `Failed to decode downloaded font: http://localhost:8080/live///fonts.gstatic.com/s/roboto/v18/KFOmCnqEu92Fr1Mu4mxK.woff2` because `OTS parsing error: invalid version tag`.

issue_title: Use boto3's default behaviour to decide the default region
issue_body:
Right now credstash uses the environment variable AWS_DEFAULT_REGION or the -r CLI parameter to specify in which region credstash should operate. If none of those are available it defaults forcibly to us-east-1 (this is hardcoded).

Boto3 will use one of the configured AWS profiles, for which a default region can be specified. Boto3 will use that region as the default when creating any client. Plus, Boto3 will use as a default the EC2 instance region when using Instance Profiles to fetch credentials.

Is there any reason to have us-east-1 hardcoded instead of relying in the default behaviour of boto3? I've already fallen trap of this a couple time by forgetting to specify a region and not realising why it was not working.

issue_title: The weak Recommends dependency is processed during recursive copies
issue_body:
### Description
Pulp should consider the [`recommends` weak forward dependency](http://rpm.org/user_doc/dependencies.html#weak-dependencies) when calculating recursive RPM copy dependencies. To give a real-world workflow use case with both positive and negative check:

- [ ] create an `F28` repository and sync it from the [Fedora28 Server](http://mirrors.nic.cz/fedora/linux/releases/28/Server/x86_64/os/) (fewer RPMs) feed
- [ ] apply the `pulp_rpm` PR https://github.com/pulp/pulp_rpm/pull/1135
- [ ] check that the `dnf` unit doesn't have the `recommends` attribute populated in Mongo
- [ ] create an `F27` repository and sync it from the [Fedora27 Server](http://mirrors.nic.cz/fedora/linux/releases/27/Server/x86_64/os/) feed
- [ ] check that there are 2 `dnf` rpm content units in Mongo
- [ ] check that the `dnf` rpm with `f27 release` has the `recommends` attribute populated and that it contains `sqlite`
- [ ] create an empty repo e.g `foo`
- [ ] copy the `dnf...-f27` rpm recursively to the `foo` repo
- [ ] check that `sqlite` rpm _was_ pulled in
- [ ] create an empty repo e.g `bar`
- [ ] copy the `dnf...-f28` rpm recursively to the `bar` repo
- [ ] check that the `sqlite` rpm _wasn't_ pulled in 

The test can be reduced to the positive branch only for the automation.

### Notes
:beetle: https://pulp.plan.io/issues/3847

Mind using your local Fedora mirror; the links above use a CZ one

from wagtail.wagtailcore.fields import StreamField

issue_title: pr_curve_streaming_op creates duplicate graphs with offset of one step
issue_body:
I believe submitting both the stats tensors and op to tf.summary.tensor_summary creates duplicate plots
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/17044118/36063743-a4cfcd78-0ebb-11e8-83bf-b2807d30f2f2.png)

```python
    def compute_summary(tp, fp, tn, fn, collections):
      precision = tp / tf.maximum(_MINIMUM_COUNT, tp + fp)
      recall = tp / tf.maximum(_MINIMUM_COUNT, tp + fn)

      return _create_tensor_summary(
          name,
          tp,
          fp,
          tn,
          fn,
          precision,
          recall,
          num_thresholds,
          display_name,
          description,
          collections)

    pr_curve = compute_summary(tp, fp, tn, fn, metrics_collections)
    update_op = compute_summary(update_tp, update_fp, update_tn, update_fn,
                                updates_collections)

    return pr_curve, update_op
```

I have made a quick fix that looks like this:
```python
    def compute_summary(tp, fp, tn, fn, collections):
      precision = tp / tf.maximum(_MINIMUM_COUNT, tp + fp)
      recall = tp / tf.maximum(_MINIMUM_COUNT, tp + fn)

      return tf.stack([
        tf.cast(tp, tf.float32),
        tf.cast(fp, tf.float32),
        tf.cast(tn, tf.float32),
        tf.cast(fn, tf.float32),
        tf.cast(precision, tf.float32),
        tf.cast(recall, tf.float32)
      ])

    pr_curve = tf.summary.tensor_summary(
      name='pr_curves',
      tensor=compute_summary(tp, fp, tn, fn, metrics_collections),
      collections=metrics_collections,
      summary_metadata=metadata.create_summary_metadata(
        display_name=display_name if display_name is not None else name,
        description=description or '',
        num_thresholds=num_thresholds
      )
    )

    update_op = compute_summary(update_tp, update_fp, update_tn, update_fn,
                                updates_collections)

    return pr_curve, update_op
```

This removes the duplicate plot but most of the code are a bad code duplication.

Will appreciate any recommendations for fix without code change or maybe a cleaner fix.

issue_title: Consuming an exclusive queue kills the coolamqp thread
crash in publishEvent with data containing non-json serializable fields
issue_body:
I've been writing a custom encoder to be able to send messages to watson iot in a custom binary format.

Line 166 in device.py does json.dumps(data) before running the data through the encoder. This means that the data you send to watson must be json serializable even if you have your own encoder that does not use json at all.

This behaviour seems undesirable, there are a two fixes i can think of:

1. replace json.dumps(data) with str(data). Smallest possible fix most objects only print "<file.class at 0x00...>"
2. move the log statement down to line 171 and log str(payload). Works for json encoders but has the same problem as point 1 for other payload types. 

If there is a consensus on the desired behaviour I will create a pull request with the fix

issue_title: GapicConfigPipeline can overwrite file in remote environment
issue_body:
### What:

GapicConfigPipeline currently raises a ValueError if the target GAPIC config file already exists. This can be disabled in a remote environment.
### Why:

This test is justified in the local environment, because GapicConfigPipeline can overwrite the GAPIC config in a developer's [`googleapis`](https://github.com/googleapis/googleapis) sandbox that might have unstaged changes. Not having this test is potentially surprising and frustrating for developers using the local environment.

For developers using a remote environment, there is no such danger. GapicConfigPipeline does not touch an active sandbox in this mode, and instead delivers a tarball of the [`googleapis`](https://github.com/googleapis/googleapis) repository with generated changes overlaid. Having this test is potentially surprising and frustrating for developers using a remote environment, because they have to know that they need to temporarily modify their artman config to re-generate a GAPIC config file.
### How:

The test is in [`pipeline.tasks.gapic_tasks.GapicConfigMoveTask._move_to()`](https://github.com/googleapis/artman/blob/master/pipeline/tasks/gapic_tasks.py#L64).

issue_title: Cython generated c files should not be part of the source distribution
issue_body:
From  a development standpoint, any auto-generated files should not be generated as they can be refreshed at build time. Cython should be made a build dependency in the `setup.py` file and c-files should be excluded from `MANIFEST.in`.

From a packaging standpoint, the build system will be called twice, one for the  Python 2 build and another one for Python 3. Each time, the c-files will be refreshed, overwriting the ones currently provided by the source distribution. This generates unnecessary pain for packaging.

issue_title: Handcrafting guide is broken while using json dumps to serialize handcrafted tx to json str
issue_body:
* bigchaindb-driver version: bigchaindb-driver==0.4.0
* Python version: Python 3.5.2
* Operating System: Ubuntu 16.04.2 LTS(Xenial)

### Description
While following the handcrafting guide for a CREATE transaction, came across an issue after creating a transaction ID right after the **Up to now** section. I was trying to serialize the **handcrafted_creation_tx** to json string as per the guide and it fails by complaining that the transaction ID is not JSON serializable. Same scenario with **prepared_creation_tx**

### What I Did

```
json_str_handcrafted_tx = json.dumps(handcrafted_creation_tx, sort_keys=True)
AND
json_str_prepared_tx = json.dumps(prepared_creation_tx, sort_keys=True)
```
###### Traceback
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/json/__init__.py", line 237, in dumps
    **kw).encode(obj)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/json/encoder.py", line 198, in encode
    chunks = self.iterencode(o, _one_shot=True)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/json/encoder.py", line 256, in iterencode
    return _iterencode(o, 0)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/json/encoder.py", line 179, in default
    raise TypeError(repr(o) + " is not JSON serializable")
TypeError: b'be9cd8825d6d7cf5f1ac81883e9c6b7c43f7ada92a4321c135c5ec5a2f9c637b' is not JSON serializable
```

###### Solution

1. One fix can be to use rapidjson for the aforementioned commands:
```
import rapidjson
json_str_handcrafted_tx = rapidjson.dumps(handcrafted_creation_tx, skipkeys=False, ensure_ascii=False, sort_keys=True)
json_str_prepared_tx = rapidjson.dumps(prepared_creation_tx, skipkeys=False, ensure_ascii=False, sort_keys=True)
```
2. Another fix can be to decode the 'id' to utf-8 before calling json.dumps
```
handcrafted_creation_tx['id'] = handcrafted_creation_tx['id'].decode('utf-8')
prepared_creation_tx['id'] = prepared_creation_tx['id'].decode('utf-8')

json_str_handcrafted_tx = json.dumps(handcrafted_creation_tx, sort_keys=True)
json_str_prepared_tx = json.dumps(prepared_creation_tx, sort_keys=True)

handcrafted_creation_tx['id'] = handcrafted_creation_tx['id'].encode('utf-8')
prepared_creation_tx['id'] = prepared_creation_tx['id'].encode('utf-8')

```

issue_title: Allow querying an account without calling new_account in ACMEv2
issue_body:
If you do this with boulder, you get:
```
  File "/Users/bmw/Development/certbot/certbot/acme/acme/client.py", line 130, in query_registration
    return self._send_recv_regr(regr, messages.UpdateRegistration())
  File "/Users/bmw/Development/certbot/certbot/acme/acme/client.py", line 75, in _send_recv_regr
    response = self._post(regr.uri, body)
  File "/Users/bmw/Development/certbot/certbot/acme/acme/client.py", line 92, in _post
    return self.net.post(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/Users/bmw/Development/certbot/certbot/acme/acme/client.py", line 1109, in post
    return self._post_once(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/Users/bmw/Development/certbot/certbot/acme/acme/client.py", line 1123, in _post_once
    return self._check_response(response, content_type=content_type)
  File "/Users/bmw/Development/certbot/certbot/acme/acme/client.py", line 983, in _check_response
    raise messages.Error.from_json(jobj)
acme.messages.Error: urn:ietf:params:acme:error:malformed :: The request message was malformed :: No Key ID in JWS header
```
This is because [querying an account requires kid to be set](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-acme-acme-13#section-6.2), but if [self.net.account isn't set, kid isn't included](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/c129ab2965f7ec4edf173b1cd900bd5add13cd2a/acme/acme/client.py#L938).

An easy fix for this would be to set `self.net.account = regr` in the beginning of `query_registration`, at least for ACMEv2. Doing it at the end of `query_registration` to store the account as given by the ACME server isn't a bad idea either.

This is useful for the scenario in #6246.

issue_title: Update tests/letstest/targets.yaml to more relevant AMIs
issue_body:
This is something I'd like to sooner rather than later as many of these tests are now irrelevant as they are testing old, sometimes EOL'd distros, and not testing more modern and popular ones. The lack of more modern tests has caused us to ship bugs.

@joohoi or especially @SwartzCr, this would be something useful for you to take off my plate.

issue_title: Post to new-authz endpoint requires Expires
issue_body: According to the ACME documentation the CA must leave the expires field in the new-autz post response empty. If that field is not present in the response certbot fails during the decoding of the response. According to the spec that field should never be there and therefore ignored.
